name: ANA Pattern
text_chunk: ANA pattern (immunofluorescence staining pattern on HEp-2 cells) helps identify the target autoantigen and guides specific antibody testing. Homogeneous pattern suggests anti-dsDNA or anti-histone (SLE, drug-induced lupus). Speckled pattern suggests anti-Sm, anti-RNP, anti-SSA/Ro, anti-SSB/La. Centromere pattern is characteristic of limited scleroderma (CREST). Nucleolar pattern suggests anti-Scl-70 (diffuse scleroderma). Pattern identification directs subsequent reflex testing.
clinical_significance: Directs specific autoantibody testing; homogeneous suggests SLE, speckled suggests mixed CTD, centromere suggests limited scleroderma.

name: ANA Screen
text_chunk: Antinuclear antibody (ANA) screen detects autoantibodies directed against nuclear components. Performed by indirect immunofluorescence (IIF) on HEp-2 cells. Positive ANA is found in systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE, >95%), drug-induced lupus, Sjogren syndrome, scleroderma, and mixed connective tissue disease. However, low-titer ANA positivity occurs in 5-15% of healthy individuals, especially elderly women. Negative ANA effectively excludes SLE (high negative predictive value). Positive results require titer and pattern characterization.
clinical_significance: Screening test for systemic autoimmune diseases; negative ANA effectively excludes SLE; positive in 5-15% of healthy individuals.

name: ANA Titer
text_chunk: ANA titer quantifies the highest serum dilution at which antinuclear antibodies remain detectable. Higher titers (>=1:160) have greater specificity for autoimmune disease. Titers of 1:40 are common in healthy individuals (up to 32%) and are not clinically significant. Titers >=1:640 are strongly suggestive of systemic autoimmune disease. Serial titer monitoring does not reliably correlate with disease activity in SLE. Titer should always be interpreted alongside ANA pattern and clinical presentation.
clinical_significance: Higher titers (>=1:160) more specific for autoimmune disease; 1:40 common in healthy individuals; serial monitoring not recommended.

name: Anti-Beta-2 GP I IgG
text_chunk: Anti-beta-2 glycoprotein I (anti-B2GP1) IgG antibodies target beta-2 glycoprotein I, a phospholipid-binding protein. Anti-B2GP1 is considered the most pathogenic antiphospholipid antibody, directly promoting thrombosis through endothelial activation, complement activation, and platelet aggregation. Part of the updated Sydney APS classification criteria. Measured by ELISA. Persistent positivity on 2 occasions 12 weeks apart is diagnostically significant.
clinical_significance: Most pathogenic antiphospholipid antibody; directly promotes thrombosis; part of updated APS classification criteria.

name: Anti-Beta-2 GP I IgM
text_chunk: Anti-beta-2 glycoprotein I IgM is part of the complete antiphospholipid antibody panel. IgM isotype has weaker clinical associations with thrombosis compared to IgG but is included in diagnostic workup. Persistent positivity should prompt clinical correlation for APS features. Measured by ELISA. Values above 20 SMU are considered positive.
clinical_significance: Part of complete APS workup; IgM isotype has weaker thrombotic association than IgG.

name: Anti-CCP
text_chunk: Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are highly specific (>95%) for rheumatoid arthritis and can be detected years before clinical disease onset. Anti-CCP positivity predicts more aggressive, erosive disease course. Present in 60-70% of RA patients. Unlike RF, anti-CCP is rarely positive in other conditions. Measured by ELISA or chemiluminescent immunoassay. Values above 19.9 U/mL are positive. Anti-CCP is now included in the 2010 ACR/EULAR RA classification criteria.
clinical_significance: Highly specific for RA (>95%); predicts erosive disease; can be positive years before symptom onset; included in ACR/EULAR criteria.

name: Anti-Cardiolipin IgG
text_chunk: Anti-cardiolipin IgG antibodies are part of the antiphospholipid antibody panel used to diagnose antiphospholipid syndrome (APS). APS causes arterial/venous thrombosis and pregnancy complications (recurrent miscarriage, preeclampsia, IUGR). Medium-to-high titer anti-cardiolipin IgG (>40 GPL) on 2 occasions 12 weeks apart is one of the laboratory criteria for APS. Measured by ELISA. Low-titer positivity may be transient and non-pathogenic.
clinical_significance: Part of APS diagnostic criteria; medium-high titers on 2 occasions predict thrombosis and pregnancy complications.

name: Anti-Cardiolipin IgM
text_chunk: Anti-cardiolipin IgM antibodies are part of the antiphospholipid antibody panel. IgM isotype is less strongly associated with thrombotic risk compared to IgG but still part of the revised Sapporo APS classification criteria. Transient IgM positivity can occur with infections (syphilis, HIV, hepatitis C). Persistent medium-high titer IgM on 2 occasions 12 weeks apart supports APS diagnosis. Measured by ELISA.
clinical_significance: Part of APS panel; IgM less thrombotic risk than IgG; transient positivity possible with infections.

name: Anti-Centromere Antibodies
text_chunk: Anti-centromere antibodies (ACA) target centromere proteins (CENP-A, CENP-B, CENP-C). Highly specific for limited cutaneous systemic sclerosis (CREST syndrome: calcinosis, Raynaud, esophageal dysmotility, sclerodactyly, telangiectasia). Present in 60-80% of limited SSc. ACA positivity is associated with better prognosis than anti-Scl-70 but carries increased risk of pulmonary arterial hypertension. Identified by characteristic centromere pattern on HEp-2 IIF.
clinical_significance: Highly specific for limited scleroderma (CREST); better prognosis than dcSSc but increased PAH risk.

name: Anti-Jo-1 Antibodies
text_chunk: Anti-Jo-1 antibodies target histidyl-tRNA synthetase and are the most common of the anti-aminoacyl-tRNA synthetase (anti-ARS) antibodies. They define the antisynthetase syndrome: inflammatory myopathy, interstitial lung disease, arthritis, mechanic's hands, Raynaud phenomenon, and fever. Present in 20-30% of polymyositis/dermatomyositis patients. Anti-Jo-1 positivity strongly predicts interstitial lung disease. Measured by ELISA or multiplex immunoassay.
clinical_significance: Defines antisynthetase syndrome; strongly predicts interstitial lung disease in myositis patients; present in 20-30% of PM/DM.

name: Anti-Mitochondrial Antibodies
text_chunk: Anti-mitochondrial antibodies (AMA) target the E2 subunit of pyruvate dehydrogenase complex on the inner mitochondrial membrane. AMA is the serological hallmark of primary biliary cholangitis (PBC), present in >90% of patients with >95% specificity. AMA can be detected years before clinical disease or elevated ALP. Measured by indirect immunofluorescence or ELISA. Positive AMA with elevated ALP is virtually diagnostic of PBC without liver biopsy.
clinical_significance: Pathognomonic for primary biliary cholangitis (>90% sensitivity, >95% specificity); can precede clinical disease by years.

name: Anti-RNP Antibodies
text_chunk: Anti-U1 RNP antibodies target U1 small nuclear ribonucleoprotein. High-titer anti-RNP is the defining serological feature of mixed connective tissue disease (MCTD). Also found in SLE (25-47%), scleroderma, and polymyositis. Anti-RNP positivity in SLE is associated with lower risk of nephritis but higher risk of Raynaud phenomenon, arthritis, and interstitial lung disease. Measured by ELISA or multiplex immunoassay.
clinical_significance: Defining marker of MCTD at high titers; in SLE associated with lower nephritis risk but higher Raynaud/arthritis/ILD risk.

name: Anti-SSA/Ro Antibodies
text_chunk: Anti-SSA/Ro antibodies target Ro52 and Ro60 ribonucleoproteins. Present in Sjogren syndrome (60-70%), subacute cutaneous lupus, neonatal lupus, and SLE. Anti-SSA is the most common extractable nuclear antigen (ENA) antibody in autoimmune disease. Critical clinical significance in pregnancy: maternal anti-SSA can cross the placenta and cause congenital heart block in the fetus (1-2% risk per pregnancy). All anti-SSA-positive women of childbearing age require preconception counseling.
clinical_significance: Associated with Sjogren, SLE, neonatal lupus; critical pregnancy risk for congenital heart block (1-2%); requires preconception counseling.

name: Anti-SSB/La Antibodies
text_chunk: Anti-SSB/La antibodies are almost always found in conjunction with anti-SSA/Ro and are highly specific for Sjogren syndrome. Isolated anti-SSB (without anti-SSA) is rare. Anti-SSB positivity in the context of anti-SSA may modulate neonatal lupus risk. Present in 40-50% of primary Sjogren syndrome and 10-15% of SLE patients. Measured by ELISA or multiplex immunoassay.
clinical_significance: Highly specific for Sjogren syndrome; almost always co-occurs with anti-SSA; may modulate neonatal lupus risk.

name: Anti-Scl-70 Antibodies
text_chunk: Anti-Scl-70 (anti-topoisomerase I) antibodies are specific for diffuse cutaneous systemic sclerosis (dcSSc). Present in 20-40% of dcSSc patients. Anti-Scl-70 positivity is associated with more severe skin involvement, pulmonary fibrosis (interstitial lung disease), and worse prognosis compared to anti-centromere antibody-positive limited scleroderma. Measured by ELISA or multiplex immunoassay.
clinical_significance: Specific for diffuse systemic sclerosis; associated with severe skin disease and pulmonary fibrosis; worse prognosis vs anti-centromere.

name: Anti-Smith Antibodies
text_chunk: Anti-Smith (anti-Sm) antibodies target small nuclear ribonucleoprotein (snRNP) and are pathognomonic for SLE (specificity ~99%). Present in only 20-30% of SLE patients but virtually absent in other conditions. Anti-Sm levels do not correlate well with disease activity. Positive anti-Sm is included in the SLICC classification criteria for SLE. Measured by ELISA or multiplex immunoassay. A positive result in the appropriate clinical context is virtually diagnostic of SLE.
clinical_significance: Pathognomonic for SLE (specificity ~99%); present in 20-30% of SLE patients; does not fluctuate with disease activity.

name: Anti-Smooth Muscle Antibodies
text_chunk: Anti-smooth muscle antibodies (ASMA) target F-actin and are associated with autoimmune hepatitis type 1 (AIH-1). Present in 70-80% of AIH-1 patients. Titers >=1:80 are clinically significant. ASMA can also be positive in chronic viral hepatitis, primary sclerosing cholangitis, and other liver diseases at lower titers. Measured by indirect immunofluorescence. ASMA combined with ANA and elevated IgG supports AIH-1 diagnosis.
clinical_significance: Associated with autoimmune hepatitis type 1 (70-80%); titers >=1:80 are clinically significant; combined with ANA and IgG supports AIH diagnosis.

name: Anti-dsDNA Antibodies
text_chunk: Anti-double-stranded DNA (anti-dsDNA) antibodies are highly specific for systemic lupus erythematosus (specificity >95%) and correlate with disease activity, especially lupus nephritis. Rising anti-dsDNA titers often precede SLE flares by weeks to months. Measured by ELISA, Farr assay, or Crithidia luciliae immunofluorescence. Present in 60-70% of SLE patients. Anti-dsDNA combined with low complement (C3/C4) is a strong predictor of renal involvement. Optimal below 5 IU/mL in healthy individuals.
clinical_significance: Highly specific for SLE (>95%); correlates with disease activity and lupus nephritis; rising titers predict flares.

name: C3 Complement
text_chunk: Complement C3 is the central component of the complement cascade, involved in opsonization, inflammation, and immune complex clearance. Low C3 indicates complement consumption from active autoimmune disease (SLE flare, lupus nephritis), immune complex-mediated disease, or hereditary complement deficiency. High C3 occurs as an acute phase response. Measured by immunoturbidimetry. Serial monitoring helps track SLE disease activity (C3 drops before clinical flare). Optimal range 100-150 mg/dL.
clinical_significance: Low C3 indicates complement consumption in active SLE/lupus nephritis; serial monitoring tracks disease activity; drops before clinical flare.

name: C4 Complement
text_chunk: Complement C4 is consumed in the classical complement pathway activated by immune complexes. Low C4 is more sensitive than low C3 for detecting classical pathway activation in SLE. C4 null alleles (C4A or C4B deficiency) are common in SLE patients and cause constitutively low C4 independent of disease activity. Measured by immunoturbidimetry. Low C4 with normal C3 suggests early or mild classical pathway activation. Both low C3 and C4 indicate severe complement consumption. Optimal range 15-35 mg/dL.
clinical_significance: More sensitive than C3 for classical pathway activation; C4 null alleles common in SLE cause baseline low C4; both low C3/C4 indicates severe consumption.

name: CH50 Total Complement
text_chunk: CH50 (total hemolytic complement) is a functional assay measuring the entire classical complement pathway (C1 through C9). A normal CH50 excludes complete deficiency of any classical pathway component. Low CH50 occurs in active SLE, serum sickness, cryoglobulinemia, or hereditary complement deficiencies. Near-zero CH50 strongly suggests complete deficiency of a complement component. Measured by hemolytic (liposome) assay. Optimal range 35-55 U/mL.
clinical_significance: Functional screen of entire classical complement pathway; normal result excludes complete component deficiency; near-zero suggests hereditary deficiency.

name: DGP-IgA
text_chunk: Deamidated gliadin peptide IgA (DGP-IgA) detects antibodies against deamidated gluten peptides. Useful as an additional celiac marker, particularly in children under 2 years where tTG-IgA may be less reliable. DGP-IgA has good sensitivity (80-95%) and specificity (90-95%) for celiac disease. Measured by ELISA.
clinical_significance: Additional celiac marker; particularly useful in children under 2 years; good sensitivity and specificity.

name: DGP-IgG
text_chunk: Deamidated gliadin peptide IgG (DGP-IgG) is used for celiac disease screening in IgA-deficient patients as an alternative to tTG-IgG. DGP-IgG has better specificity than older gliadin antibody assays. Useful when total IgA is low and tTG-IgA may be falsely negative. Measured by ELISA.
clinical_significance: Alternative celiac marker for IgA-deficient patients; better specificity than older gliadin antibody assays.

name: EMA IgA
text_chunk: Anti-endomysial antibodies IgA (EMA-IgA) are highly specific (>99%) for celiac disease. EMA targets tissue transglutaminase in the endomysium of smooth muscle. Detected by indirect immunofluorescence on monkey esophagus or human umbilical cord substrate. More labor-intensive and operator-dependent than tTG-IgA ELISA, so typically used as a confirmatory test. A positive EMA with positive tTG-IgA is essentially diagnostic of celiac disease.
clinical_significance: Highly specific (>99%) confirmatory test for celiac disease; positive EMA + tTG-IgA is essentially diagnostic.

name: IgA Total
text_chunk: Total immunoglobulin A (IgA) is the predominant antibody at mucosal surfaces (GI, respiratory, urogenital). Low IgA (selective IgA deficiency, prevalence 1:500) causes false-negative tTG-IgA in celiac screening and increases sinopulmonary infection risk. Elevated IgA occurs in IgA nephropathy, liver cirrhosis, chronic infections, and IgA myeloma. IgA deficiency is the most common primary immunodeficiency. Measured by immunoturbidimetry. Optimal 100-350 mg/dL.
clinical_significance: Must be measured with celiac screening to exclude IgA deficiency; low IgA is most common primary immunodeficiency (1:500).

name: IgE Total
text_chunk: Total immunoglobulin E (IgE) mediates type I (immediate) hypersensitivity reactions. Elevated total IgE occurs in allergic diseases (atopic dermatitis, allergic rhinitis, asthma), parasitic infections, allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis (ABPA), and hyper-IgE syndrome. Very high IgE (>2000 IU/mL) suggests ABPA or hyper-IgE syndrome. Specific IgE testing (RAST/ImmunoCAP) identifies individual allergens. Measured by chemiluminescent immunoassay. Optimal below 80 IU/mL.
clinical_significance: Elevated in atopic diseases, parasitic infections, ABPA; very high levels suggest ABPA or hyper-IgE syndrome; specific IgE identifies individual allergens.

name: IgG Total
text_chunk: Total immunoglobulin G (IgG) is the most abundant serum antibody. Elevated IgG occurs in autoimmune hepatitis (>1.5x ULN is part of diagnostic criteria), chronic infections, liver cirrhosis, IgG4-related disease, and IgG myeloma. Low IgG indicates primary or secondary immunodeficiency. IgG subclass analysis (IgG1-4) may be needed for suspected IgG4-related disease or selective subclass deficiency. Measured by immunoturbidimetry. Optimal 750-1400 mg/dL.
clinical_significance: Elevated in autoimmune hepatitis (diagnostic criterion), chronic infections, cirrhosis; low indicates immunodeficiency.

name: IgM Total
text_chunk: Total immunoglobulin M (IgM) is the first antibody produced in acute immune response and predominates in primary biliary cholangitis (PBC), where polyclonal IgM elevation is characteristic. Elevated IgM also occurs in Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia (monoclonal IgM), chronic infections, and hyper-IgM syndrome. Low IgM increases susceptibility to encapsulated bacterial infections. Measured by immunoturbidimetry. Optimal 50-200 mg/dL.
clinical_significance: Characteristically elevated in PBC; monoclonal IgM in Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia; low increases encapsulated bacterial infection risk.

name: Lupus Anticoagulant
text_chunk: Lupus anticoagulant (LA) is detected by prolongation of phospholipid-dependent coagulation assays (dRVVT screen/confirm ratio). Despite the name, LA is a prothrombotic antibody that causes thrombosis in vivo but prolongs clotting time in vitro. LA is the strongest predictor of thrombosis among the antiphospholipid antibodies. Triple positivity (LA + anti-cardiolipin + anti-B2GP1) carries the highest thrombotic risk. LA testing requires no anticoagulant therapy at time of draw.
clinical_significance: Strongest thrombotic predictor among antiphospholipid antibodies; triple positivity carries highest risk; prothrombotic in vivo despite prolonged in vitro clotting.

name: Rheumatoid Factor
text_chunk: Rheumatoid factor (RF) is an autoantibody (typically IgM) directed against the Fc portion of IgG. Present in 70-80% of rheumatoid arthritis (RA) patients but also found in other autoimmune diseases, chronic infections, and 5-10% of healthy elderly. Measured by immunoturbidimetry. Higher titers correlate with more aggressive disease, extra-articular manifestations, and worse prognosis. RF combined with anti-CCP has improved diagnostic specificity for RA (>95%). Optimal below 10 IU/mL.
clinical_significance: Present in 70-80% of RA; also positive in other autoimmune diseases and healthy elderly; combined with anti-CCP improves RA specificity to >95%.

name: tTG-IgA
text_chunk: Anti-tissue transglutaminase IgA (tTG-IgA) is the preferred serologic screening test for celiac disease (sensitivity 93-98%, specificity 95-98%). Values above 10x the upper limit of normal are virtually diagnostic without biopsy (ESPGHAN criteria in children). Must be paired with total IgA to exclude IgA deficiency (2-3% of celiac patients are IgA-deficient, causing false-negative tTG-IgA). Measured by ELISA. Optimal below 3 U/mL.
clinical_significance: Preferred celiac disease screening test (93-98% sensitivity); must check total IgA to exclude deficiency; >10x ULN virtually diagnostic.

name: tTG-IgG
text_chunk: Anti-tissue transglutaminase IgG (tTG-IgG) is used for celiac disease screening in IgA-deficient patients (2-3% of celiac patients). Lower sensitivity and specificity than tTG-IgA in IgA-sufficient patients. Should only be ordered when total IgA is low or when tTG-IgA is negative with high clinical suspicion. Measured by ELISA.
clinical_significance: Used for celiac screening in IgA-deficient patients; lower accuracy than tTG-IgA in IgA-sufficient individuals.

name: ABO Blood Type
text_chunk: ABO blood type (A, B, AB, O) is determined by carbohydrate antigens on red blood cell surfaces encoded by the ABO gene. Blood type has implications beyond transfusion medicine: type O is associated with lower von Willebrand factor and factor VIII levels (increased bleeding risk), lower VTE risk, and possibly lower cardiovascular disease risk. Type A is associated with higher VTE and stroke risk. ABO typing is essential before transfusion, organ transplant, and for paternity testing. Determined by agglutination testing.
clinical_significance: Type O associated with lower VWF/FVIII and bleeding risk; Type A associated with higher VTE/stroke risk; essential for transfusion safety.

name: Rh Factor
text_chunk: Rh factor (Rh D antigen) is the most immunogenic non-ABO red cell antigen. Rh-negative individuals exposed to Rh-positive blood (transfusion or pregnancy) develop anti-D antibodies causing hemolytic transfusion reactions or hemolytic disease of the fetus and newborn (HDFN). Rh-negative women carrying Rh-positive fetuses require anti-D immunoglobulin (RhoGAM) at 28 weeks and postpartum. Approximately 15% of Caucasians are Rh-negative. Determined by agglutination with anti-D reagent.
clinical_significance: Rh-negative women need RhoGAM in pregnancy to prevent HDFN; 15% of Caucasians are Rh-negative; critical for transfusion safety.

name: Basophils
text_chunk: Basophils are the rarest circulating granulocytes, containing histamine and heparin granules. They mediate immediate hypersensitivity reactions and play roles in allergic inflammation. Basophilia (>0.1 x10^3/uL) is uncommon and may indicate chronic myeloid leukemia, myeloproliferative neoplasms, hypothyroidism, or chronic inflammatory conditions. Persistent basophilia warrants BCR-ABL testing.
clinical_significance: Basophilia may indicate myeloproliferative neoplasm, particularly CML; basophil activation testing aids allergy diagnosis.

name: Eosinophils
text_chunk: Eosinophils are granulocytes involved in parasitic defense, allergic reactions, and immune modulation. Eosinophilia (>500/uL) is classified as mild (500-1500), moderate (1500-5000), or severe (>5000). Common causes include allergic diseases (asthma, eczema), parasitic infections, drug hypersensitivity, and eosinophilic gastrointestinal disorders. Eosinophil counts guide anti-IL-5 biologic therapy selection in severe asthma.
clinical_significance: Elevated in allergic conditions, parasitic infections, and eosinophilic disorders; guides biologic therapy selection in asthma.

name: Hematocrit
text_chunk: Hematocrit (Hct) represents the percentage of blood volume occupied by red blood cells. It parallels hemoglobin in diagnosing anemia and polycythemia but is also affected by plasma volume changes. Dehydration falsely elevates hematocrit, while fluid overload lowers it. Normal ranges are sex-dependent: males 38.3-48.6%, females 35.5-44.9%. Hematocrit is used in calculating red cell indices and is a key parameter in blood viscosity assessment.
clinical_significance: Reflects red cell mass relative to plasma volume; elevated in dehydration and polycythemia, decreased in anemia and fluid overload.

name: Hemoglobin
text_chunk: Hemoglobin (Hgb) is the oxygen-carrying protein within red blood cells. It is the most clinically useful measure for diagnosing and classifying anemia. Normal ranges differ by sex: males 13.5-17.5 g/dL, females 12.0-16.0 g/dL. Low hemoglobin triggers evaluation for iron deficiency, B12/folate deficiency, chronic disease, hemolysis, or bone marrow disorders. Critically low levels (<7 g/dL) may require transfusion. Hemoglobin is also used to assess blood loss severity and guide perioperative management.
clinical_significance: Primary marker for anemia diagnosis; critical values below 7 g/dL may require transfusion. Sex-specific reference ranges apply.

name: Lymphocyte %
text_chunk: Lymphocyte percentage represents the proportion of lymphocytes among total white blood cells. It is a component of the PhenoAge epigenetic clock algorithm developed by Levine et al. (2018). Lower lymphocyte percentage is associated with accelerated biological aging, immune senescence, and increased all-cause mortality. In the PhenoAge model, lymphocyte percentage has a negative coefficient, meaning higher values are protective. This parameter reflects overall immune competence and thymic output capacity.
clinical_significance: PhenoAge clock component; lower lymphocyte percentage indicates immune senescence and accelerated biological aging.

name: Lymphocytes
text_chunk: Lymphocytes include T cells, B cells, and natural killer (NK) cells, forming the adaptive immune system. Lymphocytosis occurs in viral infections (EBV, CMV, HIV), chronic lymphocytic leukemia, and pertussis. Lymphopenia is seen in HIV/AIDS, corticosteroid therapy, autoimmune disease, and severe sepsis. Absolute lymphocyte count is prognostic in cancer and critical illness.
clinical_significance: Elevated in viral infections and lymphoproliferative disorders; lymphopenia is prognostic in critical illness and HIV.

name: MCH
text_chunk: Mean corpuscular hemoglobin (MCH) measures the average mass of hemoglobin per red blood cell in picograms. MCH parallels MCV in anemia classification: low MCH indicates hypochromic cells (iron deficiency, thalassemia), while high MCH suggests macrocytic conditions. MCH is calculated as hemoglobin divided by RBC count. Unlike MCHC, MCH does not account for cell size, so it is most useful when interpreted alongside MCV and MCHC for comprehensive red cell characterization.
clinical_significance: Low MCH indicates hypochromic anemia (iron deficiency, thalassemia); elevated MCH parallels macrocytosis from B12 or folate deficiency.

name: MCHC
text_chunk: Mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration (MCHC) measures the average concentration of hemoglobin within red blood cells. It is the most specific index for detecting hypochromia. Low MCHC (<32 g/dL) is characteristic of iron deficiency anemia and thalassemia. Elevated MCHC (>36 g/dL) is seen in hereditary spherocytosis, where cells are hyperchromic due to membrane loss. MCHC is relatively stable and less affected by reticulocytosis than MCV or MCH.
clinical_significance: Low MCHC is specific for iron deficiency; elevated MCHC suggests hereditary spherocytosis or cold agglutinins.

name: MCV
text_chunk: Mean corpuscular volume (MCV) measures the average size of red blood cells in femtoliters. It is the primary index for morphologic classification of anemia. Microcytic anemia (MCV <80 fL) suggests iron deficiency, thalassemia, or chronic disease. Macrocytic anemia (MCV >100 fL) points to B12/folate deficiency, myelodysplasia, liver disease, or hypothyroidism. MCV is a component of the PhenoAge epigenetic clock, where higher values are associated with accelerated biological aging independent of anemia status.
clinical_significance: Classifies anemia as microcytic, normocytic, or macrocytic; elevated MCV is a PhenoAge aging biomarker independent of anemia.

name: MPV
text_chunk: Mean platelet volume (MPV) measures the average size of platelets. Larger platelets are younger and more metabolically active. Elevated MPV with low platelet count suggests peripheral destruction (ITP), where the marrow compensates by releasing larger platelets. Low MPV with thrombocytopenia suggests marrow failure. MPV is emerging as a biomarker for cardiovascular risk, as larger platelets are more prothrombotic.
clinical_significance: Elevated MPV suggests platelet destruction or cardiovascular risk; low MPV with thrombocytopenia indicates marrow suppression.

name: Monocytes
text_chunk: Monocytes are large phagocytic white blood cells that differentiate into macrophages and dendritic cells in tissues. Monocytosis occurs in chronic infections (tuberculosis, endocarditis), autoimmune diseases, inflammatory bowel disease, and certain malignancies (CMML). Monocytes play critical roles in innate immunity, antigen presentation, and tissue repair. The monocyte-to-HDL ratio is emerging as a cardiovascular risk biomarker.
clinical_significance: Elevated in chronic infections, autoimmune disease, and myeloproliferative disorders; monocyte-to-HDL ratio indicates cardiovascular risk.

name: Neutrophil-Lymphocyte Ratio
text_chunk: The neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio (NLR) is calculated by dividing the absolute neutrophil count by the absolute lymphocyte count. NLR is an integrated marker of systemic inflammation and immune balance. Elevated NLR (>3.0) is associated with worse prognosis in cardiovascular disease, cancer, sepsis, and COVID-19. NLR above 6.0 indicates significant systemic inflammation. It is inexpensive, widely available, and increasingly used as a prognostic biomarker.
clinical_significance: Elevated NLR predicts worse outcomes in cardiovascular disease, cancer, and critical illness; a low-cost inflammation marker.

name: Neutrophils
text_chunk: Neutrophils (absolute neutrophil count, ANC) are the most abundant white blood cells and the primary defense against bacterial and fungal infections. Neutrophilia occurs in bacterial infection, inflammation, stress, corticosteroid use, and myeloproliferative disorders. Neutropenia (ANC <1500/uL) increases infection risk, with severe neutropenia (<500/uL) requiring urgent evaluation. The neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio (NLR) is an emerging inflammatory and prognostic biomarker.
clinical_significance: Elevated in bacterial infection and inflammation; neutropenia below 500/uL constitutes a medical emergency with high infection risk.

name: Platelet Count
text_chunk: Platelet count measures the number of thrombocytes per microliter of blood. Platelets are essential for primary hemostasis and clot formation. Thrombocytopenia (<150,000/uL) may result from decreased production (marrow failure, chemotherapy), increased destruction (ITP, TTP, HIT, DIC), or sequestration (splenomegaly). Thrombocytosis (>400,000/uL) can be reactive (infection, inflammation, iron deficiency) or clonal (essential thrombocythemia, other myeloproliferative neoplasms).
clinical_significance: Low platelets increase bleeding risk; elevated platelets may be reactive or indicate myeloproliferative neoplasm.

name: RBC
text_chunk: Red blood cell (RBC) count quantifies the number of erythrocytes per unit volume of blood. RBCs are responsible for oxygen transport via hemoglobin. Elevated RBC (polycythemia) may be primary (polycythemia vera, JAK2 mutation) or secondary to chronic hypoxia, high altitude, or erythropoietin-producing tumors. Low RBC indicates anemia from blood loss, hemolysis, or inadequate production. The RBC count is interpreted alongside hemoglobin, hematocrit, and red cell indices (MCV, MCH, MCHC) to classify anemia type.
clinical_significance: Elevated in polycythemia vera or chronic hypoxia; decreased in anemia from blood loss, nutritional deficiency, or bone marrow disease.

name: RDW
text_chunk: Red cell distribution width (RDW) measures the variation in red blood cell size (anisocytosis). Elevated RDW indicates a mixed population of cell sizes, commonly seen in iron deficiency anemia, B12/folate deficiency, and mixed nutritional deficiencies. RDW helps distinguish iron deficiency (elevated RDW) from thalassemia trait (normal RDW). Beyond hematology, elevated RDW is an independent predictor of cardiovascular mortality and all-cause mortality. It is a component of the PhenoAge biological aging clock.
clinical_significance: Elevated RDW differentiates iron deficiency from thalassemia trait; independently predicts cardiovascular and all-cause mortality.

name: Reticulocyte Count
text_chunk: Reticulocytes are immature red blood cells released from bone marrow, containing residual RNA. The reticulocyte count reflects marrow erythropoietic activity and is essential for classifying anemia. Elevated reticulocytes (>2.5%) indicate appropriate marrow response to anemia from blood loss or hemolysis. Low reticulocytes in anemia suggest marrow failure, nutritional deficiency, or anemia of chronic disease. The reticulocyte production index corrects for anemia severity.
clinical_significance: Elevated reticulocytes indicate marrow response to blood loss or hemolysis; low reticulocytes in anemia suggest production failure.

name: WBC
text_chunk: White blood cell (WBC) count measures the total number of leukocytes in peripheral blood. It is a key indicator of immune system activity and is part of the complete blood count panel. Elevated WBC (leukocytosis) may indicate infection, inflammation, stress response, or hematologic malignancy. Low WBC (leukopenia) can signal bone marrow suppression, autoimmune conditions, or viral infections. Serial WBC trending helps monitor treatment response in infections and chemotherapy-induced neutropenia. The differential count provides additional detail about specific leukocyte populations.
clinical_significance: Elevated in infection, inflammation, stress, and leukemia; decreased in bone marrow failure, autoimmune conditions, and severe infections.

name: A/G Ratio
text_chunk: The albumin-to-globulin (A/G) ratio is calculated by dividing albumin by globulin. A low A/G ratio (<1.0) suggests overproduction of globulins (multiple myeloma, autoimmune disease) or underproduction of albumin (liver disease, malnutrition). Reversal (ratio <1.0) warrants serum protein electrophoresis, immunofixation, and further workup for monoclonal gammopathy.
clinical_significance: Reversal (ratio <1.0) warrants myeloma workup; reflects balance between liver synthetic function and immune activation.

name: Albumin
text_chunk: Serum albumin is the most abundant plasma protein, synthesized by the liver. It maintains oncotic pressure, transports hormones, drugs, and fatty acids, and serves as a nutritional and prognostic marker. Low albumin (<3.5 g/dL) indicates chronic illness, liver disease, nephrotic syndrome, malnutrition, or systemic inflammation. Albumin is a PhenoAge epigenetic clock component with a negative coefficient, meaning lower albumin strongly predicts accelerated biological aging and mortality.
clinical_significance: PhenoAge clock component; low albumin is a strong predictor of mortality and indicates chronic disease, liver dysfunction, or malnutrition.

name: BUN
text_chunk: Blood urea nitrogen (BUN) measures the amount of nitrogen in urea, a waste product of protein metabolism cleared by the kidneys. Elevated BUN occurs in renal impairment, dehydration, high protein diet, gastrointestinal bleeding, and catabolic states. Low BUN may indicate liver disease, malnutrition, or overhydration. BUN is interpreted alongside creatinine; a BUN/creatinine ratio above 20:1 suggests prerenal azotemia.
clinical_significance: Elevated in renal impairment and dehydration; BUN/creatinine ratio helps distinguish prerenal from intrinsic renal disease.

name: BUN/Creatinine Ratio
text_chunk: The BUN/creatinine ratio helps differentiate causes of elevated BUN. A ratio above 20:1 suggests prerenal azotemia from dehydration, congestive heart failure, or gastrointestinal bleeding. A ratio of 10-20:1 with both BUN and creatinine elevated indicates intrinsic renal disease. A ratio below 10:1 may indicate liver disease, low protein diet, or rhabdomyolysis.
clinical_significance: Ratio above 20:1 suggests prerenal azotemia; below 10:1 may indicate liver disease or rhabdomyolysis.

name: CO2/Bicarbonate
text_chunk: Serum CO2 (total CO2) predominantly reflects bicarbonate (HCO3-), the major blood buffer. Low CO2 indicates metabolic acidosis (diabetic ketoacidosis, lactic acidosis, renal tubular acidosis, diarrhea) or respiratory compensation for alkalosis. Elevated CO2 suggests metabolic alkalosis (vomiting, diuretics) or respiratory compensation for acidosis. Chronic low bicarbonate in CKD patients accelerates bone loss and muscle wasting.
clinical_significance: Reflects acid-base status; low CO2 indicates metabolic acidosis, elevated CO2 suggests metabolic alkalosis. Interpret with anion gap.

name: Calcium
text_chunk: Serum calcium measures total calcium (bound + ionized). Approximately 40% is protein-bound (mainly albumin), so calcium must be corrected for albumin: corrected Ca = measured Ca + 0.8 x (4.0 - albumin). Hypercalcemia (>10.5 mg/dL) is most commonly caused by primary hyperparathyroidism and malignancy. Hypocalcemia (<8.5 mg/dL) causes neuromuscular irritability, tetany, and QT prolongation.
clinical_significance: Must be corrected for albumin; hypercalcemia suggests hyperparathyroidism or malignancy, hypocalcemia indicates vitamin D deficiency or hypoparathyroidism.

name: Chloride
text_chunk: Serum chloride is the major extracellular anion, working with sodium to maintain osmolality and acid-base balance. Chloride is essential for calculating the anion gap (Na - Cl - HCO3), which helps classify metabolic acidosis. Hyperchloremia occurs in normal anion gap metabolic acidosis, dehydration, and excessive saline administration. Hypochloremia is seen in metabolic alkalosis, vomiting, and SIADH.
clinical_significance: Essential for anion gap calculation and acid-base diagnosis; hyperchloremic acidosis from saline overuse is common in ICU settings.

name: Creatinine
text_chunk: Serum creatinine is produced from creatine phosphate in skeletal muscle and is filtered by the kidneys at a relatively constant rate. It is the most widely used marker of kidney function and is used to calculate estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR). Normal ranges are sex-dependent: males 0.7-1.3 mg/dL, females 0.6-1.1 mg/dL. Creatinine is a PhenoAge clock component, where elevated levels indicate accelerated biological aging. Creatinine is affected by muscle mass, diet, and certain medications.
clinical_significance: Primary kidney function marker used to calculate eGFR; PhenoAge clock component. Affected by muscle mass and diet.

name: Globulin
text_chunk: Serum globulin is calculated as total protein minus albumin and includes alpha, beta, and gamma globulins (immunoglobulins). Elevated globulin suggests chronic inflammation, autoimmune disease, chronic infection, or plasma cell neoplasm. Low globulin may indicate immunodeficiency or nephrotic syndrome. Serum protein electrophoresis differentiates polyclonal from monoclonal gammopathy.
clinical_significance: Elevated in chronic inflammation, autoimmune disease, and multiple myeloma; low values suggest immunodeficiency.

name: Glucose (fasting)
text_chunk: Fasting blood glucose measures blood sugar after at least 8 hours without caloric intake. It is the primary screening test for diabetes mellitus. Normal fasting glucose is 70-99 mg/dL; impaired fasting glucose (pre-diabetes) is 100-125 mg/dL; diabetes is diagnosed at 126 mg/dL or above on two occasions. Fasting glucose is a component of the PhenoAge epigenetic clock, where higher levels correlate with accelerated biological aging. Hypoglycemia (<70 mg/dL) may indicate insulinoma, adrenal insufficiency, or medication effects.
clinical_significance: Primary diabetes screening marker; fasting glucose 100-125 mg/dL indicates pre-diabetes. PhenoAge clock component.

name: Lactate Dehydrogenase
text_chunk: Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) is an intracellular enzyme present in virtually all tissues. Elevated LDH indicates tissue damage or cell turnover from any cause: hemolysis, liver disease, myocardial infarction, malignancy, pulmonary embolism, or muscle injury. LDH isoenzyme fractionation can help localize the source. Measured by spectrophotometry (IFCC kinetic UV method). Normal range 120-246 IU/L, optimal below 200 IU/L. LDH is used in tumor staging (lymphoma, testicular cancer) and as a hemolysis marker.
clinical_significance: Non-specific tissue damage marker; elevated in hemolysis, liver disease, MI, malignancy; used in tumor staging.

name: Potassium
text_chunk: Serum potassium is the primary intracellular cation, critical for maintaining resting membrane potential and cardiac rhythm. Hypokalemia (<3.5 mEq/L) causes muscle weakness, arrhythmias, and rhabdomyolysis. Hyperkalemia (>5.0 mEq/L) causes peaked T waves and can lead to fatal ventricular fibrillation. Causes include renal failure, ACE inhibitors, and metabolic acidosis. Hemolyzed specimens falsely elevate results.
clinical_significance: Critical electrolyte for cardiac function; both hypo- and hyperkalemia can cause fatal arrhythmias. Check for hemolysis.

name: Sodium
text_chunk: Serum sodium is the primary extracellular cation and the major determinant of plasma osmolality and fluid balance. Hyponatremia (<136 mEq/L) is the most common electrolyte disorder in hospitalized patients, caused by SIADH, diuretics, heart failure, cirrhosis, or adrenal insufficiency. Severe hyponatremia (<120 mEq/L) causes cerebral edema and seizures. Hypernatremia (>145 mEq/L) indicates water deficit. Rapid correction of either disorder can cause osmotic demyelination.
clinical_significance: Most common electrolyte disorder; severe hyponatremia or hypernatremia is life-threatening. Correction rate must be carefully controlled.

name: Total Protein
text_chunk: Total serum protein is the sum of albumin and globulins. Elevated total protein may indicate chronic inflammation, multiple myeloma, chronic infection, or dehydration. Low total protein suggests malnutrition, nephrotic syndrome, liver disease, or protein-losing enteropathy. Serum protein electrophoresis is ordered when monoclonal gammopathy is suspected based on elevated total protein with a high globulin fraction.
clinical_significance: Elevated in myeloma and chronic inflammation; decreased in malnutrition, liver disease, and nephrotic syndrome.

name: eGFR
text_chunk: Estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR) is calculated from serum creatinine using the CKD-EPI equation. eGFR stages: normal (>90), mild decrease (60-89), moderate CKD (30-59), severe CKD (15-29), kidney failure (<15). eGFR below 60 mL/min/1.73m2 for 3+ months defines chronic kidney disease. Cystatin C-based eGFR is more accurate in extremes of muscle mass. eGFR guides drug dosing and nephrology referral timing.
clinical_significance: Primary CKD staging marker; eGFR below 60 for 3+ months defines CKD. Guides drug dosing and nephrology referral.

name: % Free PSA
text_chunk: Percent free PSA is calculated as (Free PSA / Total PSA × 100). Used to risk-stratify men with total PSA 4-10 ng/mL. Values above 25% suggest benign etiology with biopsy often deferred. Values below 10% significantly increase prostate cancer probability and typically warrant biopsy. The cutoff used varies by institution (commonly 25%). Reduces unnecessary biopsies by 20-30% while maintaining cancer detection sensitivity.
clinical_significance: >25% suggests benign disease (defer biopsy); <10% suggests cancer (proceed to biopsy); reduces unnecessary biopsies by 20-30%.

name: Free PSA
text_chunk: Free PSA measures the unbound fraction of prostate-specific antigen in serum. The ratio of free to total PSA (% Free PSA) helps distinguish benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) from prostate cancer in men with total PSA 4-10 ng/mL (the diagnostic gray zone). Free PSA is lower in prostate cancer because cancer-associated PSA binds more to protease inhibitors. A % Free PSA >25% suggests BPH, while <10% increases prostate cancer probability.
clinical_significance: Helps distinguish BPH from prostate cancer in PSA gray zone (4-10); lower free fraction suggests cancer.

name: Galleri MCED Result
text_chunk: The Galleri multi-cancer early detection (MCED) test by GRAIL uses targeted methylation-based cell-free DNA (cfDNA) sequencing to screen for over 50 cancer types from a single blood draw. It detects cancer signals from aberrant DNA methylation patterns in circulating tumor DNA, with a cancer signal origin (CSO) prediction localizing the tissue of origin. Overall sensitivity is ~51.5% across stages I-III with 99.5% specificity (0.5% false positive rate). Intended as a complement to, not replacement for, standard cancer screening.
clinical_significance: Multi-cancer blood test screening 50+ cancer types via cfDNA methylation; 99.5% specificity; complement to standard screening.

name: ADMA
text_chunk: Asymmetric dimethylarginine (ADMA) is an endogenous inhibitor of nitric oxide synthase (NOS). Elevated ADMA reduces NO bioavailability, causing endothelial dysfunction, vasoconstriction, and increased cardiovascular risk. ADMA is cleared by the enzyme DDAH and by renal excretion. Measured by LC-MS/MS from EDTA plasma. Levels above 0.64 umol/L predict cardiovascular events and all-cause mortality. Optimal range 0.30-0.55 umol/L. Exercise and dietary antioxidants may help reduce ADMA.
clinical_significance: Endogenous NOS inhibitor causing endothelial dysfunction; predicts CV events and mortality; reduced by exercise.

name: BNP
text_chunk: B-type natriuretic peptide is released in response to ventricular volume overload. Below 100 pg/mL excludes heart failure. Above 400 strongly suggests HF. Sacubitril/valsartan raises BNP but lowers NT-proBNP; use NT-proBNP for monitoring on this drug.
clinical_significance: Heart failure diagnostic marker; below 100 pg/mL excludes HF.

name: CK-MB
text_chunk: CK-MB is the cardiac isoform of creatine kinase. Primarily used to detect reinfarction because it clears faster (48-72 hours) than troponin (7-14 days). Less specific than troponin. CK-MB/total CK ratio above 5% suggests cardiac origin.
clinical_significance: Used to detect reinfarction due to faster clearance than troponin.

name: F2-Isoprostanes
text_chunk: F2-Isoprostanes (8-iso-PGF2alpha) are prostaglandin-like compounds formed by free radical-catalyzed peroxidation of arachidonic acid. They are the gold standard biomarker of in vivo oxidative stress. Measured by LC-MS/MS from urine (normalized to creatinine). Elevated F2-isoprostanes indicate systemic oxidative damage and are associated with atherosclerosis, diabetes complications, and neurodegeneration. Optimal levels below 0.5 ng/mg Cr. Antioxidant supplementation and smoking cessation can reduce levels.
clinical_significance: Gold standard oxidative stress biomarker; elevated in atherosclerosis, diabetes complications, and neurodegeneration.

name: Galectin-3
text_chunk: Galectin-3 is a beta-galactoside-binding lectin that drives cardiac fibrosis and remodeling. Elevated galectin-3 predicts heart failure hospitalization and mortality. FDA-cleared as a prognostic biomarker for heart failure alongside NT-proBNP. Measured by ELISA. Values above 17.8 ng/mL indicate increased risk of cardiac fibrosis and adverse outcomes. Useful for risk stratification in patients with established or suspected heart failure.
clinical_significance: FDA-cleared HF prognostic marker; drives cardiac fibrosis; predicts HF hospitalization and mortality alongside NT-proBNP.

name: GlycA
text_chunk: GlycA (glycoprotein acetylation) is a composite NMR-measured biomarker reflecting the glycosylation state of acute phase proteins (alpha-1 acid glycoprotein, haptoglobin, alpha-1 antitrypsin, transferrin, alpha-2 macroglobulin). GlycA integrates systemic inflammation from multiple pathways into a single measurement. Elevated GlycA predicts cardiovascular events, type 2 diabetes, and all-cause mortality independently of hs-CRP. Optimal levels below 350 umol/L. GlycA is more stable than hs-CRP (less day-to-day variation).
clinical_significance: Composite inflammation marker more stable than hs-CRP; predicts CV events, T2D, and mortality independently.

name: Lp-PLA2 Activity
text_chunk: Lipoprotein-associated phospholipase A2 (Lp-PLA2) activity (PLAC test) measures the enzymatic activity of PLA2G7 bound to LDL particles. Lp-PLA2 generates pro-inflammatory mediators (lysophosphatidylcholine, oxidized fatty acids) within the arterial intima, promoting plaque instability. FDA-cleared as a cardiovascular risk marker. Activity above 200 nmol/min/mL indicates increased risk of coronary events and stroke. Optimal below 150 nmol/min/mL. Statin therapy reduces Lp-PLA2 activity.
clinical_significance: FDA-cleared CV risk marker; promotes arterial plaque instability; reduced by statin therapy.

name: Myeloperoxidase
text_chunk: Myeloperoxidase (MPO) is a heme peroxidase enzyme released by activated neutrophils and monocytes during inflammation. Elevated MPO indicates vascular inflammation and oxidative stress in the arterial wall, promoting LDL oxidation and endothelial dysfunction. MPO is an independent predictor of major adverse cardiac events (MACE) even in troponin-negative chest pain patients. Measured by ELISA from EDTA plasma. Optimal levels below 350 pmol/L.
clinical_significance: Vascular inflammation marker; predicts MACE in troponin-negative ACS; promotes LDL oxidation and endothelial dysfunction.

name: TMAO
text_chunk: Trimethylamine N-oxide (TMAO) is a gut microbiome-derived metabolite produced from dietary choline, carnitine, and betaine. Elevated TMAO promotes atherosclerosis through enhanced macrophage cholesterol accumulation, platelet hyperreactivity, and impaired reverse cholesterol transport. Measured by LC-MS/MS with stable isotope dilution. TMAO levels above 6.2 uM are associated with increased MACE risk. Dietary modification (reducing red meat, L-carnitine) and gut microbiome interventions can lower TMAO.
clinical_significance: Gut microbiome metabolite promoting atherosclerosis; modifiable through diet and microbiome interventions.

name: Troponin I
text_chunk: High-sensitivity cardiac troponin I is the gold standard for myocardial injury and acute MI diagnosis. Released within 2-4 hours of injury. Rise/fall pattern with value above 99th percentile required for AMI diagnosis. Serial measurement at 0 and 3 hours enables rapid rule-out.
clinical_significance: Gold standard for acute MI diagnosis; rise/fall pattern required.

name: D-Dimer
text_chunk: D-Dimer is a fibrin degradation product. Below 500 ng/mL (or age-adjusted cutoff) has high negative predictive value for VTE exclusion. Elevated in VTE, DIC, surgery, trauma, pregnancy, cancer, and infection. Wells score combined with D-Dimer enables safe VTE exclusion without imaging.
clinical_significance: High negative predictive value for VTE exclusion below 500 ng/mL. Age-adjusted cutoffs improve specificity.

name: Fibrinogen (coag)
text_chunk: Fibrinogen measured functionally in coagulation assessment. Essential for clot formation; consumed in DIC. Below 100 mg/dL requires cryoprecipitate. Critical for viscoelastic testing (TEG/ROTEM) in trauma and surgical bleeding management.
clinical_significance: Essential coagulation factor; below 100 mg/dL in DIC requires cryoprecipitate.

name: PT/INR
text_chunk: Prothrombin time measures extrinsic coagulation pathway, reported as INR. Therapeutic INR for warfarin is 2.0-3.0 (most indications) or 2.5-3.5 (mechanical valves). VKORC1 and CYP2C9 genotypes significantly affect warfarin dose requirements.
clinical_significance: Monitors warfarin therapy; VKORC1/CYP2C9 genotypes guide dosing.

name: PTT
text_chunk: Partial thromboplastin time (aPTT) measures the intrinsic and common coagulation pathways (factors XII, XI, IX, VIII, X, V, II, fibrinogen). Prolonged aPTT occurs with heparin therapy, factor deficiencies (hemophilia A/B), lupus anticoagulant, and liver disease. aPTT is used to monitor unfractionated heparin therapy (target 1.5-2.5x control). Unexplained aPTT prolongation with normal PT suggests factor VIII, IX, XI, or XII deficiency or lupus anticoagulant. Optimal 26-33 seconds.
clinical_significance: Measures intrinsic pathway; monitors heparin therapy; prolonged in hemophilia and lupus anticoagulant; unexplained prolongation needs mixing study.

name: Adiponectin
text_chunk: Adiponectin is an adipokine with insulin-sensitizing, anti-inflammatory, and antiatherogenic properties. Unlike most adipokines, adiponectin decreases with increasing adiposity. Low adiponectin is associated with insulin resistance, type 2 diabetes, metabolic syndrome, NAFLD, and cardiovascular disease. Inversely correlated with visceral fat mass. Thiazolidinediones significantly raise adiponectin levels.
clinical_significance: Insulin-sensitizing adipokine; low levels predict diabetes, metabolic syndrome, and cardiovascular disease independent of BMI.

name: C-Peptide
text_chunk: C-Peptide is co-secreted with insulin in equimolar amounts but is not extracted by the liver, making it a reliable marker of endogenous insulin production. Essential for distinguishing type 1 (low/absent) from type 2 (normal/elevated) diabetes and for detecting factitious insulin use. Falling C-Peptide indicates beta-cell failure and the need for insulin therapy.
clinical_significance: Distinguishes type 1 from type 2 diabetes; measures endogenous insulin production. Falling levels indicate beta-cell failure.

name: Fasting Insulin
text_chunk: Fasting insulin measures circulating insulin after an overnight fast. It is an early marker of insulin resistance, often elevated years before glucose abnormalities appear. Optimal is below 10 uIU/mL; above 15 suggests significant insulin resistance. Combined with fasting glucose it calculates HOMA-IR. Elevated fasting insulin with normal glucose indicates compensated insulin resistance.
clinical_significance: Early marker of insulin resistance; optimal below 10 uIU/mL. Elevated with normal glucose indicates compensated resistance.

name: Fructosamine
text_chunk: Fructosamine measures glycated serum proteins (primarily albumin) and reflects average blood glucose over the preceding 2-3 weeks. Alternative to HbA1c when conditions alter red cell lifespan (hemolytic anemia, hemoglobin variants, recent transfusion, pregnancy). Also useful for monitoring rapid glycemic changes after medication adjustments. Affected by albumin levels.
clinical_significance: Alternative to HbA1c for 2-3 week glycemic control; use when hemoglobin variants or altered red cell lifespan affect HbA1c.

name: HOMA-IR
text_chunk: The Homeostatic Model Assessment of Insulin Resistance (HOMA-IR) is calculated as (fasting insulin x fasting glucose) / 405. Below 1.0 is optimal; 1.0-1.9 is early insulin resistance; 2.0-2.9 is significant; above 3.0 indicates severe insulin resistance. HOMA-IR detects metabolic dysfunction 10-15 years before diabetes diagnosis and is an independent predictor of cardiovascular events.
clinical_significance: Gold standard surrogate for insulin resistance; above 2.0 indicates significant resistance. Predicts diabetes 10-15 years before diagnosis.

name: HbA1c
text_chunk: Hemoglobin A1c (HbA1c) reflects average blood glucose over the preceding 2-3 months by measuring the percentage of glycated hemoglobin. Normal is below 5.7%; pre-diabetes is 5.7-6.4%; diabetes is diagnosed at 6.5% or above. Modified by TCF7L2 genotype. Conditions that alter red cell lifespan can falsify results. Targets are individualized: below 7% for most diabetics, below 8% for elderly.
clinical_significance: Primary diabetes monitoring marker; 5.7-6.4% indicates pre-diabetes, above 6.5% diagnoses diabetes. Modified by TCF7L2 genotype.

name: IGF-1
text_chunk: Insulin-like growth factor 1 (IGF-1) is produced primarily by the liver in response to growth hormone. Primary screening test for acromegaly (elevated) and GH deficiency (low). Age-specific reference ranges are essential. Both very low and very high IGF-1 are associated with increased mortality (U-shaped relationship). Plays roles in cancer biology and longevity pathways.
clinical_significance: Primary screening marker for acromegaly and GH deficiency; U-shaped mortality relationship. Age-specific ranges required.

name: Leptin
text_chunk: Leptin is a satiety hormone produced by adipose tissue that signals energy sufficiency to the hypothalamus. Levels correlate directly with body fat mass. Obesity causes leptin resistance. Leptin is a GrimAge epigenetic clock component, where elevated levels are associated with accelerated biological aging. Low leptin indicates lipodystrophy or severe caloric restriction.
clinical_significance: GrimAge clock component; elevated in obesity (leptin resistance), low in lipodystrophy. Reflects adipose tissue mass and metabolic aging.

name: ApoE Genotype
text_chunk: Apolipoprotein E (APOE) genotype determined by rs429358 and rs7412 SNPs defines six genotypes (E2/E2, E2/E3, E2/E4, E3/E3, E3/E4, E4/E4). The E4 allele is the strongest common genetic risk factor for late-onset Alzheimer disease (3-4x risk for E3/E4, 12-15x for E4/E4) and also increases LDL cholesterol and cardiovascular risk. E2 allele is protective against Alzheimer but increases type III hyperlipoproteinemia risk. Determined by PCR + TaqMan SNP genotyping from DNA. Results are lifelong and unchanging.
clinical_significance: Strongest common genetic AD risk factor; E4 also increases LDL/CVD risk; E2 protective for AD but raises type III hyperlipidemia risk; lifelong result.

name: ApoE Protein Level
text_chunk: Apolipoprotein E protein level in serum reflects hepatic ApoE production. ApoE is essential for lipoprotein metabolism, mediating clearance of triglyceride-rich remnant particles via LDL receptor family. ApoE levels vary by genotype: E4 carriers typically have lower ApoE levels with less efficient remnant clearance. Low ApoE protein may indicate E4 homozygosity or liver dysfunction. Measured by immunoturbidimetry. Optimal 3-6 mg/dL. Complements genotype testing by assessing functional protein expression.
clinical_significance: Reflects hepatic ApoE production; varies by genotype (E4 carriers have lower levels); complements genotype testing for functional assessment.

name: MTHFR A1298C
text_chunk: MTHFR A1298C (rs1801131) is a second common MTHFR variant with milder effect than C677T. CC homozygotes have mildly reduced enzyme activity. A1298C alone rarely causes significant hyperhomocysteinemia, but compound heterozygosity (C677T CT + A1298C AC) can cause clinically relevant enzyme reduction similar to C677T TT. Determined by PCR + TaqMan SNP genotyping. Compound heterozygotes may benefit from methylfolate supplementation.
clinical_significance: Milder effect than C677T alone; compound heterozygosity (677CT + 1298AC) clinically significant; may benefit from methylfolate.

name: MTHFR C677T
text_chunk: MTHFR C677T (rs1801133) is the most clinically significant MTHFR variant. CT heterozygotes have ~65% enzyme activity; TT homozygotes have ~30% activity with increased homocysteine levels. The T allele frequency is 25-35% in European populations. TT homozygotes require methylfolate supplementation (not folic acid, which requires MTHFR for activation). MTHFR status affects neural tube defect risk, cardiovascular risk, and response to methotrexate therapy. Determined by PCR + TaqMan SNP genotyping.
clinical_significance: CT: ~65% activity; TT: ~30% activity with elevated homocysteine; TT requires methylfolate (not folic acid); affects NTD risk and methotrexate response.

name: AMH
text_chunk: Anti-Mullerian hormone (AMH) is produced by granulosa cells of preantral and small antral ovarian follicles. AMH is the best biomarker of ovarian reserve, remaining relatively stable throughout the menstrual cycle. Low AMH (<1.0 ng/mL) indicates diminished ovarian reserve with reduced response to ovarian stimulation in IVF. Very low AMH (<0.1 ng/mL) suggests approaching menopause. In males, AMH assesses Sertoli cell function. Female ref: 0.9-9.5 ng/mL. Male ref: 0.7-19.0 ng/mL. Measured by electrochemiluminescent immunoassay.
clinical_significance: Best ovarian reserve biomarker; cycle-independent; low AMH predicts poor IVF response; very low suggests approaching menopause.

name: Adrenomedullin
text_chunk: Adrenomedullin (ADM) is a vasodilatory peptide from vascular endothelium and adrenal medulla. GrimAge epigenetic clock component associated with accelerated aging. Biomarker of endothelial dysfunction, sepsis severity, and cardiovascular disease. Predicts mortality in heart failure and sepsis.
clinical_significance: GrimAge clock component and endothelial dysfunction marker; predicts mortality in sepsis and heart failure.

name: Cortisol
text_chunk: Cortisol is the primary glucocorticoid. Morning cortisol below 3 ug/dL suggests adrenal insufficiency; above 18 excludes it. Cushing syndrome screened with 24h urinary free cortisol, late-night salivary cortisol, or dexamethasone suppression test. Chronic elevation contributes to visceral obesity and insulin resistance.
clinical_significance: Primary stress hormone; morning cortisol below 3 ug/dL suggests adrenal insufficiency. Chronic elevation causes metabolic syndrome.

name: Cystatin C
text_chunk: Cystatin C is a cysteine protease inhibitor produced at constant rate by all nucleated cells. Alternative to creatinine for GFR estimation, unaffected by muscle mass, diet, or sex. GrimAge epigenetic clock component where elevated levels predict accelerated biological aging independent of kidney function.
clinical_significance: GrimAge clock component; muscle-mass-independent GFR marker.

name: DHEA-S
text_chunk: DHEA-S is the most abundant circulating steroid hormone. Peaks in mid-20s and declines ~10% per decade (adrenopause). Low levels associated with aging, cardiovascular disease, and cognitive decline. Used to evaluate adrenal androgen excess and insufficiency. No significant diurnal variation.
clinical_significance: Declines with age; low levels associated with aging and cardiovascular risk.

name: DHT
text_chunk: Dihydrotestosterone (DHT) is the most potent androgen, formed from testosterone by 5-alpha reductase (types 1 and 2). DHT mediates prostate growth, male pattern hair loss, acne, and hirsutism. In males, elevated DHT is associated with BPH and androgenetic alopecia. 5-alpha reductase inhibitors (finasteride, dutasteride) reduce DHT by 60-90% to treat BPH and hair loss. In females, elevated DHT contributes to PCOS-related hirsutism. Male ref: 14-77 ng/dL. Measured by LC-MS/MS.
clinical_significance: Most potent androgen; elevated in BPH and androgenetic alopecia; target of 5-alpha reductase inhibitors; elevated in PCOS-related hirsutism.

name: Estradiol
text_chunk: Estradiol (E2) is the primary estrogen. In women, varies with menstrual cycle. Low estradiol causes amenorrhea, osteoporosis, and vasomotor symptoms. In men, below 10-20 pg/mL causes osteoporosis; above 40-50 pg/mL causes gynecomastia.
clinical_significance: Primary estrogen; extremes cause bone loss (low) or gynecomastia (high) in men.

name: FSH
text_chunk: Follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) is a pituitary gonadotropin that stimulates folliculogenesis in women and spermatogenesis in men. Elevated FSH indicates primary gonadal failure (menopause, premature ovarian insufficiency, Klinefelter syndrome). Low FSH suggests hypothalamic-pituitary dysfunction. In women, day-3 FSH >10-12 mIU/mL indicates diminished ovarian reserve. FSH rises during perimenopause before menstrual irregularity. Male ref: 1.5-12.4 mIU/mL. Female follicular ref: 3.5-12.5 mIU/mL. Postmenopausal: >25 mIU/mL.
clinical_significance: Elevated in primary gonadal failure/menopause; day-3 FSH assesses ovarian reserve; rises in perimenopause before clinical symptoms.

name: Free Testosterone
text_chunk: Free testosterone is the biologically active (unbound) fraction of total testosterone, comprising only 1-3% of total testosterone. The remainder is bound to SHBG (60-70%) and albumin (30-40%). Free testosterone is the most clinically relevant measure when SHBG is altered (obesity lowers SHBG falsely elevating free T; OCP/aging raises SHBG falsely lowering free T). Male reference: 6.8-21.5 pg/mL (optimal 10-20). Female reference: 0.3-4.1 pg/mL (optimal 0.5-3.0). Measured by equilibrium dialysis + LC-MS/MS (gold standard).
clinical_significance: Most clinically relevant testosterone measure when SHBG is altered; sex-specific ranges critical; equilibrium dialysis is gold standard method.

name: LH
text_chunk: Luteinizing hormone (LH) is a pituitary gonadotropin that triggers ovulation and corpus luteum formation in women and stimulates Leydig cell testosterone production in men. LH surge at mid-cycle triggers ovulation. Elevated baseline LH with elevated LH/FSH ratio (>2:1) suggests polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS). Elevated LH in men indicates primary hypogonadism (testicular failure). Male ref: 1.7-8.6 mIU/mL. Female follicular: 2.4-12.6 mIU/mL. Mid-cycle surge: 14-96 mIU/mL.
clinical_significance: Triggers ovulation; elevated LH/FSH ratio >2:1 suggests PCOS; elevated in primary hypogonadism; sex and cycle-phase dependent.

name: NT-proBNP
text_chunk: NT-proBNP is released by cardiomyocytes in response to ventricular wall stress. Primary biomarker for heart failure diagnosis, prognosis, and monitoring. Below 125 pg/mL in non-acute settings excludes HF. Age-specific cutoffs apply. Affected by renal function and obesity.
clinical_significance: Primary heart failure biomarker; below 125 pg/mL excludes chronic HF.

name: PSA
text_chunk: Prostate-specific antigen (PSA) below 4.0 ng/mL is generally normal. PSA velocity, density, and free/total ratio improve specificity. Also elevated in BPH, prostatitis, and after prostate manipulation. 5-alpha reductase inhibitors lower PSA by ~50%.
clinical_significance: Prostate cancer screening marker; PSA velocity and free/total ratio improve specificity.

name: Progesterone
text_chunk: Progesterone is produced by the corpus luteum after ovulation. Mid-luteal progesterone above 3 ng/mL confirms ovulation; above 10 ng/mL indicates adequate luteal function. Low levels associated with anovulation and recurrent miscarriage.
clinical_significance: Confirms ovulation when above 3 ng/mL; low levels associated with anovulation and recurrent miscarriage.

name: Prolactin
text_chunk: Prolactin is a pituitary hormone primarily involved in lactation. Elevated prolactin (hyperprolactinemia) causes galactorrhea, amenorrhea, and infertility in women; hypogonadism and erectile dysfunction in men. Causes include prolactinoma (pituitary adenoma), medications (antipsychotics, metoclopramide), hypothyroidism, and renal failure. Macroprolactin (biologically inactive IgG-bound prolactin) can cause falsely elevated results; PEG precipitation identifies macroprolactinemia. Male ref: 4-15.2 ng/mL. Female ref: 4.8-23.3 ng/mL.
clinical_significance: Elevated causes galactorrhea/amenorrhea/infertility; common causes include prolactinoma and medications; macroprolactin causes false elevations.

name: SHBG
text_chunk: Sex hormone binding globulin (SHBG) is a glycoprotein that binds and transports sex steroids (testosterone, estradiol, DHT). SHBG determines the bioavailability of sex hormones. Elevated SHBG (from OCP use, hyperthyroidism, liver disease, aging) reduces free testosterone. Low SHBG (from obesity, insulin resistance, hypothyroidism, androgens) increases free testosterone. Male reference: 16.5-55.9 nmol/L. Female reference: 24.6-122 nmol/L. Measured by immunoassay.
clinical_significance: Determines sex hormone bioavailability; elevated by OCP/aging/liver disease; reduced by obesity/insulin resistance; sex-specific ranges.

name: TIMP1
text_chunk: Tissue inhibitor of metalloproteinase 1 (TIMP1) regulates extracellular matrix remodeling. GrimAge epigenetic clock component where elevated levels predict accelerated aging. Associated with fibrosis, cancer progression, and cardiovascular disease.
clinical_significance: GrimAge clock component; elevated in fibrosis, cancer, and cardiovascular disease.

name: Testosterone (Total)
text_chunk: Total testosterone is measured in the morning (8-10 AM). Normal adult male range 264-916 ng/dL. Hypogonadism defined as below 300 ng/dL with symptoms. Associated with fatigue, low libido, erectile dysfunction, osteoporosis, and metabolic syndrome. Declines 1-2% per year after age 30.
clinical_significance: Primary male sex hormone; below 300 ng/dL with symptoms defines hypogonadism.

name: CD4+ T Cells
text_chunk: CD4+ T cells (helper T lymphocytes) orchestrate adaptive immune responses through cytokine secretion and immune cell activation. CD4 count is the primary marker for monitoring HIV disease progression and ART response. CD4 below 200 cells/uL defines AIDS and triggers opportunistic infection prophylaxis. In non-HIV contexts, low CD4 occurs in idiopathic CD4 lymphopenia, immunosuppressive therapy, and some autoimmune diseases. Measured by flow cytometry (CD3+CD4+). Optimal 600-1500 cells/uL.
clinical_significance: Primary HIV monitoring marker; <200 defines AIDS; also relevant in immunosuppression and autoimmune disease.

name: CD4/CD8 Ratio
text_chunk: The CD4/CD8 ratio reflects the balance between helper and cytotoxic T lymphocytes. Inverted ratio (<1.0) occurs in HIV, chronic viral infections, immunosenescence, and some autoimmune conditions. A very low ratio in aging populations is associated with increased mortality (immune risk phenotype). High ratios (>3.5) may indicate T-cell lymphoma or certain autoimmune diseases. The ratio is more informative than individual counts for assessing immune homeostasis. Optimal 1.2-3.0.
clinical_significance: Reflects immune balance; inverted ratio (<1.0) in HIV, infections, immunosenescence; associated with mortality in elderly.

name: CD8+ T Cells
text_chunk: CD8+ T cells (cytotoxic T lymphocytes) kill virus-infected cells and tumor cells through perforin/granzyme-mediated cytotoxicity. Elevated CD8 occurs in acute viral infections (CMV, EBV, HIV), chronic viral hepatitis, and some autoimmune conditions. Low CD8 is seen in some primary immunodeficiencies. The CD4/CD8 ratio provides additional immune balance information. Measured by flow cytometry (CD3+CD8+). Optimal 250-1000 cells/uL.
clinical_significance: Cytotoxic lymphocytes; elevated in viral infections; important for immune balance assessment via CD4/CD8 ratio.

name: NK Cells
text_chunk: Natural killer (NK) cells (CD16+CD56+) provide innate immune surveillance against viral infections and malignant cells without prior antigen sensitization. Low NK cell counts or function increase susceptibility to viral infections and certain cancers. Elevated NK cells occur in acute stress, chronic infections, and some NK-cell lymphoproliferative disorders. NK cell activity assays (cytotoxicity testing) provide functional assessment beyond enumeration. Measured by flow cytometry. Optimal 100-500 cells/uL.
clinical_significance: Innate immune surveillance against viruses and cancer; low counts increase infection/cancer susceptibility.

name: ESR
text_chunk: Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) is a nonspecific inflammation marker. ESR above 100 mm/hr strongly suggests serious pathology: malignancy, infection (endocarditis, osteomyelitis), autoimmune disease (giant cell arteritis), or myeloma. Primary monitoring marker for giant cell arteritis and polymyalgia rheumatica.
clinical_significance: Nonspecific inflammation marker; ESR above 100 mm/hr suggests serious pathology. Primary monitoring marker for giant cell arteritis.

name: Fibrinogen
text_chunk: Fibrinogen is a coagulation factor (Factor I) and acute phase reactant. Elevated fibrinogen is an independent cardiovascular risk factor, increasing blood viscosity and platelet aggregation. Low fibrinogen (<100 mg/dL) indicates DIC, liver failure, or congenital afibrinogenemia. Above 700 mg/dL significantly increases thrombotic risk.
clinical_significance: Acute phase reactant and coagulation factor; elevated levels increase cardiovascular and thrombotic risk. Low levels suggest DIC.

name: GDF-15
text_chunk: Growth differentiation factor 15 (GDF-15) is a stress-responsive TGF-beta superfamily cytokine and one of the strongest biomarkers of biological aging and all-cause mortality. Core component of the GrimAge epigenetic clock. Levels increase exponentially with age, chronic disease, cancer, heart failure, and mitochondrial dysfunction. Also mediates cancer cachexia.
clinical_significance: GrimAge clock component and strongest aging biomarker; elevated in cancer, heart failure, and mitochondrial dysfunction.

name: Homocysteine
text_chunk: Homocysteine is a sulfur-containing amino acid in methionine metabolism. Elevated homocysteine (>15 umol/L) is an independent cardiovascular risk factor associated with VTE, cognitive decline, and osteoporosis. MTHFR C677T (rs1801133) is the most common genetic cause, reducing enzyme activity 30% (het) to 70% (hom). B-vitamin supplementation lowers levels but has not reduced CV events in trials.
clinical_significance: Cardiovascular risk factor modified by MTHFR genotype; elevated in B12, folate, or B6 deficiency.

name: IL-6
text_chunk: Interleukin-6 (IL-6) is a pleiotropic pro-inflammatory cytokine that stimulates hepatic CRP production and drives the acute phase response. Elevated in aging (inflammaging), obesity, cardiovascular disease, and cancer. Levels rise exponentially with age. Tocilizumab (anti-IL-6R antibody) treats rheumatoid arthritis and cytokine release syndrome.
clinical_significance: Key pro-inflammatory cytokine driving CRP production; elevated in aging, obesity, and severe inflammation.

name: PAI-1
text_chunk: Plasminogen activator inhibitor-1 (PAI-1) is the primary inhibitor of tissue plasminogen activator, regulating fibrinolysis. Elevated PAI-1 promotes prothrombotic state and is associated with cardiovascular disease, metabolic syndrome, and obesity. GrimAge epigenetic clock component. The 4G/5G SERPINE1 promoter polymorphism affects expression levels.
clinical_significance: GrimAge clock component; promotes prothrombotic state. Elevated in metabolic syndrome and visceral obesity.

name: TNF-alpha
text_chunk: Tumor necrosis factor alpha (TNF-alpha) is a master pro-inflammatory cytokine produced primarily by macrophages. Elevated in rheumatoid arthritis, IBD, psoriasis, and obesity. Anti-TNF therapies (infliximab, adalimumab, etanercept) revolutionized autoimmune disease treatment. Chronic low-grade elevation contributes to inflammaging, insulin resistance, and sarcopenia.
clinical_significance: Master inflammatory cytokine; chronic elevation contributes to inflammaging and insulin resistance.

name: hs-CRP
text_chunk: High-sensitivity C-reactive protein (hs-CRP) measures low-level systemic inflammation and is an independent cardiovascular risk predictor. Risk categories: low <1.0, moderate 1.0-3.0, high >3.0 mg/L. JUPITER trial demonstrated statin benefit with elevated hs-CRP and normal LDL. PhenoAge epigenetic clock component where elevated levels strongly predict accelerated biological aging and mortality.
clinical_significance: PhenoAge clock component and cardiovascular risk predictor; above 3.0 mg/L indicates high risk.

name: Ferritin
text_chunk: Ferritin is the primary intracellular iron storage protein and most sensitive single test for iron deficiency. Below 30 ng/mL is diagnostic (sensitivity 92%, specificity 98%). Also an acute phase reactant elevated in inflammation. In inflammatory states, below 100 ng/mL with TSAT below 20% suggests iron deficiency. Above 1000 ng/mL warrants HFE genotyping.
clinical_significance: Most sensitive iron deficiency marker; below 30 ng/mL diagnostic. Also an acute phase reactant elevated in inflammation.

name: Serum Iron
text_chunk: Serum iron measures circulating iron bound to transferrin. Has significant diurnal variation (highest in morning) and is affected by recent iron intake. Low serum iron occurs in iron deficiency, chronic inflammation, and blood loss. Elevated in hemochromatosis, transfusional siderosis, and liver disease. Best interpreted alongside ferritin, TIBC, and transferrin saturation.
clinical_significance: Measures circulating iron; significant diurnal variation limits isolated utility. Interpret with ferritin and transferrin saturation.

name: Soluble Transferrin Receptor
text_chunk: Soluble transferrin receptor (sTfR) is shed from cell surfaces in proportion to cellular iron demand. Unlike ferritin, sTfR is not affected by inflammation, making it valuable for diagnosing iron deficiency with concurrent chronic disease. The sTfR/ferritin ratio is the most accurate method for distinguishing iron deficiency anemia from anemia of chronic disease.
clinical_significance: Not affected by inflammation; best test for iron deficiency in chronic disease. sTfR/ferritin ratio distinguishes IDA from ACD.

name: TIBC
text_chunk: Total iron-binding capacity (TIBC) measures the maximum amount of iron transferrin can bind, reflecting transferrin concentration. Elevated in iron deficiency; low in iron overload, chronic inflammation, and liver disease. Used to calculate transferrin saturation (TSAT = serum iron / TIBC x 100).
clinical_significance: Reflects transferrin levels; elevated in iron deficiency, decreased in iron overload and inflammation.

name: Transferrin Saturation
text_chunk: Transferrin saturation (TSAT) is calculated as (serum iron / TIBC) x 100. Below 20% indicates iron deficiency and is the threshold for IV iron therapy in heart failure and CKD. Above 45% suggests iron overload and triggers HFE genotyping for hemochromatosis. In HFE C282Y homozygotes, elevated TSAT is often the earliest finding, preceding ferritin elevation by years.
clinical_significance: Below 20% indicates iron deficiency; above 45% triggers hemochromatosis evaluation. Earliest marker of HFE-related iron overload.

name: Microalbumin/Creatinine Ratio
text_chunk: Urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio (UACR) detects microalbuminuria, the earliest marker of diabetic nephropathy. Normal below 30 mg/g; moderately increased 30-300; severely increased above 300. Annual screening recommended in diabetes and hypertension. ACE inhibitors and ARBs reduce albuminuria.
clinical_significance: Earliest marker of diabetic nephropathy; annual screening recommended in diabetes and hypertension.

name: Phosphorus
text_chunk: Serum phosphorus is regulated by kidneys, PTH, and FGF23. Hyperphosphatemia in CKD drives cardiovascular calcification and mortality. Hypophosphatemia in refeeding syndrome is life-threatening. FGF23 is the primary hormonal regulator.
clinical_significance: Hyperphosphatemia in CKD drives cardiovascular calcification; hypophosphatemia in refeeding syndrome is life-threatening.

name: Uric Acid
text_chunk: Uric acid is the end product of purine metabolism. Hyperuricemia (>7.0 men, >6.0 women) causes gout. Associated with hypertension, cardiovascular disease, CKD, and metabolic syndrome. Very high levels with chemotherapy indicate tumor lysis syndrome risk.
clinical_significance: Elevated in gout, CKD, and metabolic syndrome; high levels indicate tumor lysis syndrome risk during chemotherapy.

name: ApoB
text_chunk: Apolipoprotein B (ApoB) is the structural protein on all atherogenic lipoprotein particles. Since each particle carries one ApoB molecule, ApoB directly measures atherogenic particle number. ApoB is superior to LDL-C for cardiovascular risk assessment when particle number and cholesterol content are discordant. Optimal ApoB is below 90 mg/dL; below 65 for high-risk patients. Does not require fasting.
clinical_significance: Directly measures atherogenic particle number; superior to LDL for cardiovascular risk. Optimal below 90 mg/dL.

name: Ceramides Score
text_chunk: The ceramide risk score (1-12 scale) is based on plasma concentrations of specific ceramide species (Cer16:0, Cer18:0, Cer24:1) and their ratios. Ceramides are bioactive sphingolipids that promote inflammation, apoptosis, and insulin resistance in arterial walls. Measured by LC-MS/MS. Scores above 6 indicate high cardiovascular risk. The ceramide score predicts major adverse cardiovascular events independently of LDL-C and other traditional risk factors.
