clinical_significance: Novel CV risk biomarker based on sphingolipid species; predicts MACE independently of traditional lipid markers.

name: HDL
text_chunk: High-density lipoprotein (HDL) cholesterol mediates reverse cholesterol transport. HDL below 40 mg/dL in men and below 50 in women is an independent cardiovascular risk factor. Mendelian randomization studies show genetically elevated HDL does not reduce cardiovascular events. HDL functionality (cholesterol efflux capacity) may matter more than absolute levels. Low HDL is part of metabolic syndrome criteria.
clinical_significance: Low HDL is a cardiovascular risk factor; raising HDL pharmacologically has not reduced events. Functionality matters more than level.

name: HDL Particle Number
text_chunk: HDL particle number (HDL-P) measured by NMR spectroscopy quantifies the concentration of HDL particles independent of cholesterol content. HDL-P may be a better predictor of cardiovascular risk than HDL cholesterol (HDL-C) because it reflects reverse cholesterol transport capacity. Low HDL-P with normal HDL-C suggests dysfunctional HDL with reduced atheroprotection. Optimal HDL-P above 35 umol/L is associated with reduced cardiovascular events.
clinical_significance: Better CV risk predictor than HDL-C alone; low HDL-P with normal HDL-C indicates dysfunctional HDL particles.

name: LDL
text_chunk: Low-density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol is the primary atherogenic lipoprotein and the primary target of cardiovascular risk reduction therapy. Optimal LDL is below 100 mg/dL; below 70 for high-risk patients; below 55 for very high-risk per ESC guidelines. LDL is strongly modified by APOE genotype, PCSK9 variants, and LDLR mutations. Statin therapy reduces LDL 30-50%; PCSK9 inhibitors provide additional 50-60% reduction.
clinical_significance: Primary target for cardiovascular risk reduction; optimal below 100 mg/dL. Strongly modified by APOE and PCSK9 genotype.

name: LDL Particle Number
text_chunk: LDL particle number (LDL-P) measured by NMR lipoprofile directly quantifies LDL particles. LDL-P can be discordant with LDL-C in up to 25% of patients, particularly those with metabolic syndrome who have small dense LDL. When discordant, LDL-P better predicts cardiovascular events. Optimal LDL-P is below 1000 nmol/L.
clinical_significance: Directly measures LDL particle count; superior to LDL-C when discordant. Identifies residual risk in metabolic syndrome.

name: LDL Particle Size
text_chunk: LDL particle size (peak diameter) measured by NMR spectroscopy distinguishes Pattern A (large buoyant, >20.5 nm) from Pattern B (small dense, <20.5 nm). Pattern B is associated with 3x increased coronary heart disease risk. Particle size is influenced by triglyceride metabolism, insulin resistance, and CETP activity. Optimal size above 21 nm indicates predominantly large buoyant LDL with lower atherogenic potential.
clinical_significance: Pattern B (small dense, <20.5 nm) carries 3x CHD risk vs Pattern A (large buoyant); modifiable through triglyceride reduction.

name: Large HDL Particles
text_chunk: Large HDL particles are the subfraction most associated with effective reverse cholesterol transport and atheroprotection. Measured by NMR spectroscopy. Higher levels of large HDL indicate mature, cholesterol-laden particles actively removing cholesterol from arterial walls. Optimal levels above 7 umol/L. Low large HDL with elevated small HDL suggests impaired HDL maturation, often seen with CETP variants or metabolic syndrome.
clinical_significance: Most atheroprotective HDL subfraction; reflects effective reverse cholesterol transport capacity.

name: Large VLDL Particles
text_chunk: Large VLDL particles are triglyceride-rich lipoproteins secreted by the liver. Elevated large VLDL indicates hepatic triglyceride overproduction, a hallmark of insulin resistance and metabolic syndrome. Measured by NMR spectroscopy. Values above 2.7 nmol/L are associated with increased cardiovascular risk and atherogenic dyslipidemia. Large VLDL drives the formation of small dense LDL through lipoprotein remodeling. Optimal levels below 1.5 nmol/L.
clinical_significance: Marker of hepatic triglyceride overproduction; drives formation of atherogenic small dense LDL particles.

name: Lp(a)
text_chunk: Lipoprotein(a) is a genetically determined LDL-like particle with an additional apolipoprotein(a) chain. Levels above 50 mg/dL (125 nmol/L) confer significant cardiovascular and aortic stenosis risk. Lp(a) is 80-90% genetically determined by the LPA gene. Not significantly affected by diet, exercise, or statins. PCSK9 inhibitors lower Lp(a) by 20-30%. Lifetime testing once is sufficient.
clinical_significance: Genetically determined cardiovascular risk factor; above 50 mg/dL significantly increases risk. Test once in a lifetime.

name: Non-HDL Cholesterol
text_chunk: Non-HDL cholesterol (total cholesterol minus HDL) captures all atherogenic lipoproteins (LDL, VLDL, IDL, Lp(a)). It is a better predictor of cardiovascular risk than LDL alone, especially with high triglycerides. Non-HDL goals are 30 mg/dL above corresponding LDL goals. No fasting required. Recommended as secondary lipid target by AHA/ACC guidelines.
clinical_significance: Better cardiovascular predictor than LDL in hypertriglyceridemia; captures all atherogenic particles. No fasting required.

name: Oxidized LDL
text_chunk: Oxidized LDL (OxLDL) is a modified form of LDL that is a key initiator of atherosclerosis. OxLDL is taken up by macrophage scavenger receptors, forming foam cells in the arterial wall. Measured by ELISA. Elevated OxLDL indicates increased oxidative stress and atherogenic activity independent of LDL-C levels. Optimal levels below 50 U/L. Antioxidant intake (vitamin E, polyphenols) and statin therapy can reduce OxLDL.
clinical_significance: Key initiator of atherosclerosis through foam cell formation; reflects oxidative stress and atherogenic activity beyond LDL-C.

name: Remnant Cholesterol
text_chunk: Remnant cholesterol represents cholesterol carried by triglyceride-rich remnant lipoproteins (VLDL remnants and IDL). Calculated as Total Cholesterol minus LDL minus HDL. Elevated remnant cholesterol is an independent causal risk factor for coronary heart disease and all-cause mortality, as demonstrated by Mendelian randomization studies. Optimal levels below 18 mg/dL. Remnant cholesterol captures atherogenic risk missed by LDL-C alone.
clinical_significance: Causal risk factor for CHD per Mendelian randomization; captures atherogenic risk not reflected in LDL-C alone.

name: Small Dense LDL Particles
text_chunk: Small dense LDL (sdLDL) particles are a subfraction of LDL cholesterol that are more atherogenic due to increased arterial wall penetration, greater oxidative susceptibility, and prolonged circulation time. Measured by NMR spectroscopy (LipoProfile). Values above 527 nmol/L indicate Pattern B (small, dense) phenotype with increased cardiovascular risk independent of total LDL-C. sdLDL is particularly elevated in metabolic syndrome, insulin resistance, and hypertriglyceridemia. Optimal levels below 400 nmol/L are associated with reduced coronary event risk.
clinical_significance: More atherogenic LDL subfraction; elevated in metabolic syndrome and insulin resistance; independent CV risk predictor beyond LDL-C.

name: Total Cholesterol
text_chunk: Total cholesterol measures the sum of LDL, HDL, and VLDL cholesterol. Desirable is below 200 mg/dL; borderline high 200-239; high is 240+ mg/dL. Total cholesterol alone is insufficient for cardiovascular risk assessment because it does not distinguish atherogenic from protective fractions. It is a component of the Framingham Risk Score. Familial hypercholesterolemia should be suspected when total cholesterol exceeds 310 mg/dL.
clinical_significance: Initial cardiovascular screening marker; must be interpreted with LDL and HDL fractions for accurate risk assessment.

name: Total/HDL Ratio
text_chunk: The total cholesterol to HDL ratio is a simple cardiovascular risk metric. Below 5.0 is desirable; optimal is below 3.5. It reflects balance between atherogenic and antiatherogenic lipoproteins and is a component of the Framingham Risk Score. Lifestyle modifications (exercise, weight loss, Mediterranean diet) improve this ratio effectively.
clinical_significance: Cardiovascular risk ratio; optimal below 3.5. Component of Framingham Risk Score.

name: Triglycerides
text_chunk: Triglycerides are the main form of stored fat and a major energy source. Desirable levels below 150 mg/dL; very high 500+ mg/dL carries pancreatitis risk. Elevated triglycerides are associated with metabolic syndrome, insulin resistance, type 2 diabetes, and cardiovascular disease. Significantly affected by the GCKR rs1260326 variant. Omega-3 fatty acids and fibrates are primary therapies.
clinical_significance: Elevated triglycerides indicate metabolic syndrome and pancreatitis risk above 500 mg/dL; modified by GCKR genotype.

name: VLDL
text_chunk: Very low-density lipoprotein (VLDL) cholesterol is a triglyceride-rich lipoprotein produced by the liver. VLDL is typically estimated as triglycerides divided by 5 (Friedewald equation, valid when TG <400 mg/dL). Elevated VLDL is atherogenic and contributes to residual cardiovascular risk beyond LDL. VLDL remnants promote foam cell formation in arterial walls.
clinical_significance: Atherogenic triglyceride-rich lipoprotein; elevated in insulin resistance and metabolic syndrome.

name: ALP
text_chunk: Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) is present in liver, bone, intestine, kidney, and placenta. Elevated ALP with elevated GGT confirms hepatobiliary origin (cholestasis, biliary obstruction). Elevated ALP with normal GGT suggests bone origin (Paget disease, osteomalacia). ALP is a component of the PhenoAge epigenetic clock, where elevated values are associated with accelerated biological aging and increased mortality risk.
clinical_significance: PhenoAge clock component; elevated with GGT confirms cholestatic liver disease. Isolated elevation suggests bone disease.

name: ALT
text_chunk: Alanine aminotransferase (ALT) is a liver-specific enzyme that catalyzes transamination in hepatocytes. Elevated ALT indicates hepatocellular injury and is more specific for liver disease than AST. Common causes include viral hepatitis, NAFLD/NASH, drug-induced liver injury (acetaminophen), and autoimmune hepatitis. ALT above 10x ULN suggests acute hepatitis. The PNPLA3 rs738409 GG genotype doubles ALT levels in NAFLD.
clinical_significance: Most specific marker for hepatocellular injury; mild elevations common in NAFLD. Genotype at PNPLA3 significantly modifies ALT levels.

name: AST
text_chunk: Aspartate aminotransferase (AST) is found in liver, heart, muscle, kidney, and brain. Elevated AST is less specific for liver disease than ALT. AST/ALT ratio above 2:1 suggests alcoholic liver disease (De Ritis ratio). AST above 10x ULN indicates acute hepatitis, ischemic hepatitis, or drug toxicity. Isolated AST elevation without ALT elevation suggests cardiac or skeletal muscle source.
clinical_significance: Less liver-specific than ALT; AST/ALT ratio above 2:1 suggests alcoholic hepatitis. Elevated alone may indicate cardiac or muscle injury.

name: Direct Bilirubin
text_chunk: Direct (conjugated) bilirubin measures the water-soluble fraction processed by the liver. Elevated direct bilirubin indicates impaired biliary excretion from hepatocellular disease, intrahepatic cholestasis, or extrahepatic biliary obstruction. Causes include gallstones, pancreatic cancer, primary biliary cholangitis, and drug-induced cholestasis.
clinical_significance: Elevated in biliary obstruction and cholestatic liver disease; urgent imaging needed when combined with obstructive symptoms.

name: FIB-4 Index
text_chunk: The FIB-4 index is a non-invasive fibrosis score calculated from age, AST, ALT, and platelet count: (Age x AST) / (Platelets x sqrt(ALT)). FIB-4 below 1.3 has a 90% negative predictive value for excluding advanced fibrosis. FIB-4 above 2.67 has a 97% specificity for confirming advanced fibrosis. FIB-4 is recommended by AASLD and EASL as the first-line non-invasive fibrosis assessment in NAFLD.
clinical_significance: Non-invasive fibrosis score; below 1.3 excludes advanced fibrosis, above 2.67 confirms it. First-line NAFLD fibrosis assessment.

name: GGT
text_chunk: Gamma-glutamyl transferase (GGT) is a biliary enzyme used to confirm the hepatic origin of elevated ALP. GGT is highly sensitive for hepatobiliary disease but lacks specificity, as it is induced by alcohol, many medications, and metabolic syndrome. Elevated GGT is an independent predictor of cardiovascular mortality and type 2 diabetes. Serial GGT monitoring is used to assess alcohol abstinence.
clinical_significance: Confirms hepatic origin of elevated ALP; independent cardiovascular risk predictor. Sensitive marker for alcohol use.

name: Total Bilirubin
text_chunk: Total bilirubin is the sum of unconjugated (indirect) and conjugated (direct) bilirubin. Elevated total bilirubin causes jaundice when above 2.5-3.0 mg/dL. Unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia results from hemolysis or Gilbert syndrome (UGT1A1 polymorphism, affecting 5-10% of population). Conjugated hyperbilirubinemia indicates hepatocellular disease or biliary obstruction. Total bilirubin is part of the MELD score.
clinical_significance: Elevated in hemolysis, liver disease, and biliary obstruction; Gilbert syndrome is a benign cause. Component of MELD score.

name: Alpha-1 Globulin
text_chunk: Alpha-1 globulin fraction on SPEP primarily contains alpha-1 antitrypsin (AAT) and alpha-1 acid glycoprotein (orosomucoid). Low alpha-1 fraction suggests alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency (AATD, SERPINA1 gene mutations), which causes early-onset emphysema and liver disease. Elevated alpha-1 occurs as an acute phase response. AATD affects 1 in 2,500-5,000 individuals. Optimal 0.20-0.40 g/dL.
clinical_significance: Low suggests alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency (emphysema/liver disease); elevated in acute phase response.

name: Alpha-2 Globulin
text_chunk: Alpha-2 globulin fraction contains haptoglobin, alpha-2 macroglobulin, and ceruloplasmin. Elevated alpha-2 occurs in nephrotic syndrome (alpha-2 macroglobulin increases as smaller proteins are lost in urine), acute inflammation, and adrenal insufficiency. Low alpha-2 may indicate hemolysis (haptoglobin consumption) or Wilson disease (low ceruloplasmin). Optimal 0.50-0.90 g/dL.
clinical_significance: Elevated in nephrotic syndrome and acute inflammation; low in hemolysis (haptoglobin consumption) and Wilson disease.

name: Beta Globulin
text_chunk: Beta globulin fraction contains transferrin, complement C3, beta-lipoprotein, and beta-2 microglobulin. Elevated beta fraction occurs in iron deficiency anemia (increased transferrin), monoclonal IgA or IgM (beta-migrating M-spikes), and hypercholesterolemia. Beta-gamma bridging pattern on SPEP is characteristic of cirrhosis. Optimal 0.65-1.00 g/dL.
clinical_significance: Elevated in iron deficiency (transferrin), beta-migrating M-spikes, hyperlipidemia; beta-gamma bridging suggests cirrhosis.

name: Gamma Globulin
text_chunk: Gamma globulin fraction contains immunoglobulins (IgG, IgA, IgM, IgD, IgE). Polyclonal increase (broad gamma elevation) indicates chronic infection, autoimmune disease, or chronic inflammation. Monoclonal spike within gamma region indicates plasma cell neoplasm (myeloma, MGUS). Decreased gamma (hypogammaglobulinemia) indicates primary or secondary immunodeficiency. Absent gamma fraction is seen in X-linked agammaglobulinemia. Optimal 0.70-1.40 g/dL.
clinical_significance: Polyclonal elevation in chronic infection/autoimmune disease; monoclonal spike in myeloma/MGUS; decreased in immunodeficiency.

name: M-Spike
text_chunk: M-spike (monoclonal protein) on SPEP is a discrete band representing a single immunoglobulin produced by a clonal plasma cell population. M-spike detection requires immunofixation electrophoresis (IFE) for isotype identification (IgG kappa, IgA lambda, etc.). M-spike <1.5 g/dL with no end-organ damage is classified as MGUS (monoclonal gammopathy of undetermined significance), which has ~1% annual progression rate to myeloma. M-spike >3 g/dL raises concern for multiple myeloma.
clinical_significance: Detected monoclonal protein requires IFE characterization; <1.5 g/dL likely MGUS (1%/year myeloma progression); >3 g/dL suggests myeloma.

name: SPEP
text_chunk: Serum protein electrophoresis (SPEP) separates serum proteins into albumin, alpha-1, alpha-2, beta, and gamma globulin fractions by capillary zone electrophoresis. SPEP detects monoclonal proteins (M-spikes) indicating plasma cell dyscrasias (multiple myeloma, MGUS, Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia). Normal polyclonal pattern shows a broad gamma region. A discrete band (M-spike) requires immunofixation electrophoresis (IFE) for characterization. SPEP also detects chronic inflammation (polyclonal gammopathy) and nephrotic syndrome (low albumin, elevated alpha-2).
clinical_significance: Screens for monoclonal gammopathies (myeloma, MGUS); detects M-spikes requiring IFE characterization; identifies chronic inflammation patterns.

name: Free T3
text_chunk: Free triiodothyronine (FT3) is the most metabolically active thyroid hormone, primarily produced by peripheral conversion of T4 to T3 by deiodinase enzymes. Essential for diagnosing T3 thyrotoxicosis. Low FT3 with normal T4 (euthyroid sick syndrome) occurs in critical illness. The DIO2 rs225014 variant impairs T4-to-T3 conversion, causing some patients to respond better to combination T4/T3 therapy.
clinical_significance: Most active thyroid hormone; diagnoses T3 thyrotoxicosis. DIO2 genotype affects T4-to-T3 conversion and therapy response.

name: Free T4
text_chunk: Free thyroxine (FT4) measures the unbound, biologically active fraction of T4. Combined with TSH it provides primary thyroid function assessment. Low FT4 with elevated TSH confirms primary hypothyroidism; low FT4 with low/normal TSH suggests secondary hypothyroidism. Not affected by binding protein changes that alter total T4.
clinical_significance: Measures biologically active thyroid hormone; combined with TSH for complete thyroid assessment.

name: Free Thyroxine Index
text_chunk: Free Thyroxine Index (FTI) is calculated as Total T4 × T3 Uptake to estimate free T4 when direct free T4 measurement may be unreliable (pregnancy, severe illness, TBG abnormalities). FTI corrects for TBG variation that affects total T4. Largely superseded by direct free T4 immunoassays in routine practice but still useful when free T4 results are discordant with clinical presentation. Optimal 1.5-4.0.
clinical_significance: Estimates free T4 corrected for TBG variation; useful when direct free T4 is unreliable (pregnancy, severe illness, TBG abnormalities).

name: Reverse T3
text_chunk: Reverse T3 (rT3) is the metabolically inactive isomer of T3, produced by type 3 deiodinase from T4. Elevated rT3 occurs in nonthyroidal illness (sick euthyroid syndrome), where the body shunts T4 metabolism toward inactive rT3. Characteristic of critical illness, caloric restriction, chronic stress, and selenium deficiency.
clinical_significance: Elevated in nonthyroidal illness (sick euthyroid syndrome); reflects metabolic conservation.

name: T3 Uptake
text_chunk: T3 uptake (T3RU) is an indirect measure of thyroxine-binding globulin (TBG) saturation, not a direct measure of T3. Low T3 uptake indicates high TBG (pregnancy, OCP, hepatitis) meaning more binding sites are available. High T3 uptake indicates low TBG (nephrotic syndrome, androgens) or TBG saturation from hyperthyroidism. T3 uptake is used to calculate the Free Thyroxine Index (FTI = T4 × T3 Uptake). Optimal 28-35%.
clinical_significance: Indirect measure of TBG saturation, not T3 levels; used to calculate FTI; low in high-TBG states (pregnancy, OCP).

name: T3/rT3 Ratio
text_chunk: The T3 to reverse T3 (rT3) ratio reflects the balance between active thyroid hormone (T3) and its inactive metabolite (rT3). Low T3/rT3 ratio (<6) suggests euthyroid sick syndrome (non-thyroidal illness syndrome), where the body preferentially converts T4 to rT3 instead of active T3 during critical illness, caloric restriction, or chronic stress. This is a protective metabolic adaptation. DIO2 genotype affects T4-to-T3 conversion efficiency. Optimal ratio >6.
clinical_significance: Low ratio suggests euthyroid sick syndrome or impaired T4-to-T3 conversion; DIO2 genotype modulates conversion efficiency.

name: TBG
text_chunk: Thyroxine-binding globulin (TBG) is the primary transport protein for thyroid hormones in serum (binds ~70% of circulating T4 and T3). Elevated TBG (from estrogen/OCP, pregnancy, hepatitis, genetic TBG excess) raises total T4/T3 without affecting free hormone levels or thyroid function. Low TBG (from androgens, nephrotic syndrome, glucocorticoids, genetic TBG deficiency) lowers total T4/T3 without true hypothyroidism. Understanding TBG status prevents misinterpretation of total thyroid hormone levels. Optimal 15-30 ug/mL.
clinical_significance: Primary thyroid hormone carrier; elevated by estrogen/OCP/pregnancy affecting total T4; key to preventing misdiagnosis of thyroid dysfunction.

name: TSH
text_chunk: Thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) is the most sensitive screening test for thyroid dysfunction. Inversely related to thyroid hormone levels via negative feedback. Elevated TSH indicates hypothyroidism; suppressed TSH indicates hyperthyroidism. Subclinical hypothyroidism (TSH 4.0-10.0 with normal FT4) affects 5-10% of adults. Optimal TSH is 0.5-2.5 mIU/L. Biotin supplements can interfere with TSH assays.
clinical_significance: Most sensitive thyroid screening test; elevated indicates hypothyroidism, suppressed indicates hyperthyroidism. Optimal 0.5-2.5 mIU/L.

name: Thyroglobulin
text_chunk: Thyroglobulin (Tg) is the precursor protein for thyroid hormone synthesis stored in thyroid follicles. Serum Tg is primarily used as a tumor marker after thyroidectomy for differentiated thyroid cancer (DTC): detectable Tg after total thyroidectomy suggests persistent or recurrent disease. In the non-thyroidectomy setting, Tg levels reflect thyroid mass and TSH stimulation. TgAb positivity invalidates Tg measurement. Measured by immunoassay. Normal 1.5-38.5 ng/mL. Post-thyroidectomy target: undetectable.
clinical_significance: Primary tumor marker for differentiated thyroid cancer after thyroidectomy; detectable post-thyroidectomy suggests recurrence; invalidated by TgAb.

name: Thyroglobulin Antibodies
text_chunk: Thyroglobulin antibodies (TgAb) target thyroglobulin, the thyroid hormone precursor stored in follicular colloid. TgAb are found in Hashimoto thyroiditis (50-80%), Graves disease (50-70%), and thyroid cancer follow-up (interfere with thyroglobulin tumor marker measurement). TgAb positivity can indicate early thyroid autoimmunity before clinical hypothyroidism develops. In thyroid cancer surveillance, TgAb >0.9 IU/mL invalidates thyroglobulin measurements. Measured by chemiluminescent immunoassay.
clinical_significance: Marker of thyroid autoimmunity (Hashimoto's, Graves); interferes with thyroglobulin cancer marker; >0.9 IU/mL requires serial TgAb trending instead.

name: Thyroid Peroxidase Antibodies
text_chunk: Thyroid peroxidase (TPO) antibodies are autoantibodies present in 90% of Hashimoto thyroiditis and 75% of Graves disease patients. With subclinical hypothyroidism, they predict progression to overt hypothyroidism at approximately 4% per year. Found in 10-15% of the general population. Associated with thyroid cancer risk and pregnancy complications.
clinical_significance: Diagnostic for autoimmune thyroid disease; positive with subclinical hypothyroidism predicts 4%/year progression to overt hypothyroidism.

name: Total T4
text_chunk: Total thyroxine (T4) measures both protein-bound and free T4. Affected by thyroid-binding globulin levels, which increase in pregnancy, estrogen therapy, and liver disease. Free T4 is preferred for clinical decision-making. Total T4 remains useful in monitoring amiodarone-induced thyroid dysfunction.
clinical_significance: Affected by binding protein changes; free T4 is preferred. Useful for monitoring amiodarone-related thyroid dysfunction.

name: Arsenic Urine
text_chunk: Urine arsenic (total) measures recent arsenic exposure. Inorganic arsenic (from contaminated water, rice, occupational exposure) is a class 1 carcinogen causing skin, lung, and bladder cancer. Organic arsenic from seafood (arsenobetaine) is non-toxic and can elevate total arsenic; speciation testing distinguishes organic from inorganic forms. Levels above 50 ug/L warrant speciation and source investigation. Measured by ICP-MS. Optimal below 30 ug/L.
clinical_significance: Inorganic arsenic is a class 1 carcinogen; >50 ug/L requires speciation testing; seafood can cause benign organic arsenic elevation.

name: Cadmium Blood
text_chunk: Blood cadmium reflects recent cadmium exposure, primarily from cigarette smoke and industrial sources. Cadmium accumulates in the kidney with a biological half-life of 10-30 years, causing proximal tubular damage (Fanconi syndrome) and osteomalacia. Cadmium is a class 1 carcinogen (lung cancer). Blood cadmium >2 ug/L in non-smokers warrants occupational/environmental investigation. Measured by ICP-MS or graphite furnace AAS. Optimal below 1 ug/L.
clinical_significance: Primarily from smoking/industry; accumulates in kidneys (30-year half-life); causes tubular damage, osteomalacia; class 1 carcinogen.

name: Lead Blood
text_chunk: Blood lead level reflects recent lead exposure. No safe level of lead has been identified. CDC reference value for adults is <3.5 ug/dL (updated 2021). Elevated lead causes neurotoxicity, nephrotoxicity, anemia (basophilic stippling), and hypertension. Occupational exposure (construction, smelting, battery manufacturing) is the primary adult source. Children are more susceptible due to developing nervous system. Measured by ICP-MS or graphite furnace AAS. Optimal below 2 ug/dL.
clinical_significance: No safe level exists; >3.5 ug/dL requires source identification; causes neurotoxicity, nephrotoxicity, anemia; children especially susceptible.

name: Mercury Blood
text_chunk: Blood mercury reflects recent methylmercury exposure primarily from seafood consumption. Methylmercury is a potent neurotoxin that bioaccumulates in the food chain. Levels above 5 ug/L warrant dietary counseling to reduce high-mercury fish (swordfish, shark, king mackerel, tilefish). Levels above 10 ug/L may cause neurological symptoms (paresthesias, tremor, cognitive impairment). Measured by ICP-MS or cold vapor atomic absorption. Optimal below 3 ug/L.
clinical_significance: Reflects methylmercury from seafood; >5 ug/L requires dietary modification; >10 ug/L may cause neurological symptoms.

name: Urine RBC
text_chunk: Urine red blood cells (hematuria) above 3 per high-power field indicate bleeding from anywhere in the urinary tract. Glomerular hematuria (dysmorphic RBCs, RBC casts) suggests glomerulonephritis. Non-glomerular hematuria (isomorphic RBCs) suggests stones, infection, malignancy, or prostatic disease. Microscopic hematuria in adults over 40 requires cystoscopy and imaging to exclude bladder cancer. Exercise-induced hematuria is transient and benign.
clinical_significance: >3/HPF requires evaluation; dysmorphic RBCs suggest glomerulonephritis; adults >40 need cystoscopy to exclude malignancy.

name: Urine Specific Gravity
text_chunk: Urine specific gravity measures the concentration of dissolved solutes reflecting the kidney's ability to concentrate urine. Low values (<1.005) indicate dilute urine from excessive fluid intake, diabetes insipidus, or renal tubular damage. High values (>1.030) indicate concentrated urine from dehydration, SIADH, or heavy proteinuria/glycosuria. Fixed specific gravity at 1.010 (isosthenuria) indicates loss of concentrating ability, seen in chronic kidney disease. Measured by refractometry. Optimal 1.010-1.025.
clinical_significance: Reflects renal concentrating ability; fixed 1.010 indicates CKD; low in diabetes insipidus; high in dehydration.

name: Urine WBC
text_chunk: Urine white blood cells (pyuria) above 5 per high-power field indicate urinary tract inflammation, most commonly from infection (UTI). Sterile pyuria (WBC present without bacteria) occurs in partially treated UTI, renal tuberculosis, interstitial nephritis, nephrolithiasis, or genitourinary malignancy. Pyuria combined with positive nitrite and leukocyte esterase strongly suggests bacterial UTI. Measured by microscopy or automated urinalysis.
clinical_significance: >5/HPF indicates urinary tract inflammation; sterile pyuria requires evaluation for TB, interstitial nephritis, or malignancy.

name: Urine pH
text_chunk: Urine pH reflects acid-base balance and dietary composition. Acidic urine (pH <5.5) is normal on meat-heavy diets and occurs in metabolic acidosis, diabetic ketoacidosis, and respiratory acidosis. Alkaline urine (pH >7.0) occurs with vegetarian diets, urinary tract infections (urease-producing organisms), renal tubular acidosis type I and II, and systemic alkalosis. Urine pH affects stone formation: uric acid stones form in acidic urine, struvite and calcium phosphate stones in alkaline urine. Optimal 5.5-7.0.
clinical_significance: Reflects acid-base status and diet; affects kidney stone type; persistently alkaline may indicate UTI or renal tubular acidosis.

name: AA/EPA Ratio
text_chunk: The arachidonic acid (AA) to eicosapentaenoic acid (EPA) ratio is a specific marker of inflammatory eicosanoid balance. AA is the precursor to pro-inflammatory prostaglandins (PGE2), thromboxanes (TXA2), and leukotrienes (LTB4), while EPA produces anti-inflammatory resolvins and protectins. Ratios above 5 indicate pro-inflammatory dominance. Measured by gas chromatography from RBC membranes. Optimal below 3. Ratio is highly responsive to EPA/DHA supplementation (typically improves within 8-12 weeks).
clinical_significance: Specific marker of inflammatory eicosanoid balance; >5 indicates pro-inflammatory dominance; rapidly responsive to omega-3 supplementation.

name: Chromium
text_chunk: Chromium is a trace mineral that may enhance insulin signaling through chromodulin activation. Deficiency is rare but may impair glucose tolerance. Supplementation studies show mixed results for glycemic control. Elevated chromium indicates occupational exposure (welding, chrome plating) and can cause lung cancer, kidney damage, and allergic dermatitis. Measured by ICP-MS from serum. Clinical utility of routine chromium testing is limited.
clinical_significance: May enhance insulin signaling; deficiency rare; elevated indicates occupational exposure; clinical utility of testing is limited.

name: CoQ10
text_chunk: Coenzyme Q10 (ubiquinone) is a lipid-soluble antioxidant essential for mitochondrial electron transport chain function. Statin therapy reduces CoQ10 synthesis by inhibiting the shared mevalonate pathway (HMG-CoA reductase produces both cholesterol and CoQ10). Low CoQ10 may contribute to statin-associated myopathy. Supplementation (100-200 mg/day) is commonly recommended with statin use. Measured by HPLC from serum. Optimal 0.8-2.0 mg/L.
clinical_significance: Depleted by statin therapy (shared synthesis pathway); low levels may contribute to statin myopathy; supplementation recommended with statins.

name: Copper
text_chunk: Copper is required for iron metabolism (ceruloplasmin), connective tissue formation (lysyl oxidase), and antioxidant defense (superoxide dismutase). Wilson disease (ATP7B mutations) causes toxic copper accumulation. Excess zinc supplementation can induce copper deficiency.
clinical_significance: Required for iron metabolism; Wilson disease causes toxic accumulation. Zinc supplementation can induce copper deficiency.

name: Copper/Zinc Ratio
text_chunk: The copper/zinc ratio reflects the balance between these antagonistic trace minerals. Elevated ratio (>1.0) indicates relative copper excess or zinc deficiency, associated with oxidative stress, inflammation, impaired immune function, and accelerated aging. High Cu/Zn ratios are found in chronic inflammation, infection, cardiovascular disease, and neurodegeneration. Zinc supplementation may help normalize the ratio. Optimal 0.7-1.0.
clinical_significance: Elevated ratio indicates oxidative stress and inflammation; associated with CV disease and neurodegeneration; normalizable with zinc supplementation.

name: Folate
text_chunk: Serum folate is essential for one-carbon metabolism, DNA synthesis, and neural tube development. Deficiency causes megaloblastic anemia, elevated homocysteine, and neural tube defects. MTHFR C677T impairs conversion to active 5-methyltetrahydrofolate, requiring methylfolate supplementation rather than folic acid.
clinical_significance: Deficiency causes megaloblastic anemia and neural tube defects; MTHFR genotype determines optimal supplementation form.

name: Folate RBC
text_chunk: Red blood cell (RBC) folate reflects tissue folate stores over the preceding 120 days (RBC lifespan), providing a more stable measure than serum folate which fluctuates with recent dietary intake. Low RBC folate (<280 ng/mL) confirms folate deficiency even when serum folate is normal. Critical for MTHFR variant carriers who may have normal serum folate but impaired cellular folate metabolism. Measured by chemiluminescent immunoassay from whole blood. Optimal 400-700 ng/mL.
clinical_significance: More stable than serum folate (reflects 120-day stores); critical for confirming deficiency in MTHFR carriers with normal serum folate.

name: Iodine
text_chunk: Urinary iodine is the primary marker for iodine status. Essential for thyroid hormone synthesis. Deficiency affects 2 billion people globally causing goiter and hypothyroidism. Both deficiency and excess cause thyroid dysfunction.
clinical_significance: Essential for thyroid hormone synthesis; both deficiency and excess cause thyroid dysfunction.

name: Magnesium (RBC)
text_chunk: Red blood cell magnesium is more accurate than serum magnesium for total body status. Magnesium is essential for 600+ enzymatic reactions, ATP production, DNA repair, and neuromuscular function. Deficiency is estimated in 50-80% of the US population and is associated with hypertension, arrhythmias, insulin resistance, and migraines.
clinical_significance: RBC magnesium is more accurate than serum for total body status; deficiency is extremely common and associated with cardiometabolic disease.

name: Manganese
text_chunk: Manganese is a cofactor for mitochondrial superoxide dismutase (MnSOD), arginase, and pyruvate carboxylase. Deficiency is rare in humans. Toxicity (manganism) from occupational inhalation causes parkinsonism-like neurotoxicity. Elevated blood manganese in non-occupational settings may indicate liver disease (impaired biliary excretion) or parenteral nutrition without manganese monitoring. Measured by ICP-MS from whole blood. Optimal 0.5-1.0 ug/L.
clinical_significance: Essential cofactor for MnSOD; toxicity causes parkinsonism; elevated in liver disease (impaired biliary excretion).

name: Methylmalonic Acid
text_chunk: Methylmalonic acid (MMA) is a functional biomarker of vitamin B12 (cobalamin) status at the tissue level. Elevated MMA indicates functional B12 deficiency even when serum B12 is within the normal range (functional B12 deficiency). MMA is more specific for B12 deficiency than homocysteine (which also rises with folate deficiency). Levels above 378 nmol/L suggest B12 deficiency requiring supplementation. Renal impairment can also elevate MMA. Measured by LC-MS/MS from serum. Optimal below 250 nmol/L.
clinical_significance: Most specific functional marker of B12 deficiency; elevated when serum B12 is falsely normal; renal impairment can also elevate.

name: Omega-3 Index
text_chunk: The Omega-3 Index measures EPA + DHA as a percentage of total RBC fatty acids. Above 8% is cardiovascular protective; below 4% indicates high risk. FADS1 rs174547 significantly affects omega-3 and omega-6 levels by altering desaturase activity. The REDUCE-IT trial showed CV benefit of high-dose EPA.
clinical_significance: Cardiovascular risk marker; above 8% is protective, below 4% high risk. FADS1 genotype modifies omega-3 metabolism.

name: Omega-6/Omega-3 Ratio
text_chunk: The omega-6 to omega-3 fatty acid ratio reflects the balance between pro-inflammatory (omega-6, arachidonic acid) and anti-inflammatory (omega-3, EPA/DHA) fatty acids in red blood cell membranes. Western diets typically have ratios of 15-20:1, far exceeding the optimal ratio of <4:1. High ratios promote systemic inflammation and are associated with cardiovascular disease, autoimmune disorders, and cancer. Measured by gas chromatography from RBC membranes. Optimal below 3:1.
clinical_significance: Reflects pro-inflammatory vs anti-inflammatory fatty acid balance; Western diets typically 15-20:1; optimal <4:1; modifiable through diet.

name: Selenium
text_chunk: Selenium is incorporated into selenoproteins including glutathione peroxidases and thioredoxin reductases. Critical for thyroid function (deiodinase enzymes require selenium) and immune function. Both deficiency and excess are harmful.
clinical_significance: Essential for thyroid function and antioxidant defense; deficiency impairs T4-to-T3 conversion.

name: Vitamin A
text_chunk: Vitamin A (retinol) is essential for vision, immune function, reproduction, and cellular differentiation. Deficiency causes night blindness and immune impairment. Toxicity causes hepatotoxicity and teratogenicity. BCMO1 rs12934922 reduces beta-carotene to retinol conversion by 30-60%, meaning carriers require preformed vitamin A.
clinical_significance: Deficiency causes night blindness; toxicity causes liver damage. BCMO1 genotype affects carotene conversion.

name: Vitamin B1 Thiamine
text_chunk: Vitamin B1 (thiamine) is essential for carbohydrate metabolism and neuronal function. Deficiency causes beriberi (wet: cardiac failure; dry: peripheral neuropathy) and Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome in alcoholics. Thiamine deficiency is common in chronic alcoholism, bariatric surgery patients, prolonged ICU stays, and refeeding syndrome. Measured by LC-MS/MS from whole blood. Whole blood thiamine diphosphate (TDP) is the preferred functional marker. Optimal 80-150 nmol/L.
clinical_significance: Deficiency causes beriberi and Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome; common in alcoholism, bariatric surgery, refeeding; whole blood TDP is preferred marker.

name: Vitamin B12
text_chunk: Vitamin B12 (cobalamin) is essential for DNA synthesis, methylation, and neurological function. Deficiency below 200 pg/mL causes megaloblastic anemia and irreversible neuropathy. Functional deficiency at 200-400 pg/mL detected by elevated MMA and homocysteine. Causes include pernicious anemia, metformin use, PPI use, and vegan diet.
clinical_significance: Deficiency causes megaloblastic anemia and irreversible neuropathy; functional deficiency at 200-400 pg/mL requires MMA testing.

name: Vitamin B2 Riboflavin
text_chunk: Vitamin B2 (riboflavin) is a precursor to FAD and FMN coenzymes essential for energy metabolism, cellular function, and MTHFR enzyme activity. Riboflavin deficiency (ariboflavinosis) causes angular stomatitis, glossitis, dermatitis, and normocytic anemia. Riboflavin is required for MTHFR function; supplementation can lower homocysteine in MTHFR 677TT homozygotes. Measured by HPLC with fluorometric detection from plasma. Optimal 7-30 nmol/L.
clinical_significance: Essential for MTHFR enzyme function; supplementation lowers homocysteine in MTHFR 677TT carriers; deficiency causes stomatitis and anemia.

name: Vitamin B6
text_chunk: Vitamin B6 (pyridoxal 5-phosphate) is a cofactor in over 100 enzymatic reactions including amino acid metabolism, neurotransmitter synthesis, and homocysteine metabolism. Low B6 causes elevated homocysteine, peripheral neuropathy, depression, and impaired immunity. Excessive supplementation (>200 mg/day) can cause sensory neuropathy.
clinical_significance: Cofactor in 100+ reactions; deficiency causes neuropathy and elevated homocysteine.

name: Vitamin C
text_chunk: Vitamin C (ascorbic acid) is an essential water-soluble antioxidant required for collagen synthesis, immune function, and iron absorption. Deficiency (<0.4 mg/dL) causes scurvy (bleeding gums, poor wound healing, petechiae). Suboptimal levels are common in smokers, alcoholics, and elderly with restricted diets. Measured by HPLC with electrochemical detection from EDTA plasma. Specimen must be processed quickly as vitamin C degrades rapidly. Optimal 0.8-1.8 mg/dL.
clinical_significance: Essential antioxidant; deficiency causes scurvy; common in smokers and elderly; specimen degrades rapidly requiring prompt processing.

name: Vitamin D (25-OH)
text_chunk: 25-hydroxyvitamin D is the primary circulating form and best marker of vitamin D status. Deficiency below 20 ng/mL; insufficiency 20-29; sufficiency 30-100 ng/mL. Affects 1 billion people globally. Associated with osteoporosis, autoimmune disease, cardiovascular disease, and cancer risk. VDR and GC genotypes significantly modify requirements and supplementation response.
clinical_significance: Most common nutritional deficiency globally; below 20 ng/mL indicates deficiency. VDR and GC genotypes modify requirements.

name: Vitamin E
text_chunk: Vitamin E (alpha-tocopherol) is the primary lipid-soluble antioxidant. Deficiency is rare but causes ataxia, neuropathy, and hemolytic anemia in fat malabsorption syndromes. Levels should be lipid-adjusted in hyperlipidemia.
clinical_significance: Primary lipid-soluble antioxidant; deficiency causes ataxia and neuropathy in malabsorption.

name: Vitamin K1
text_chunk: Vitamin K1 (phylloquinone) is essential for synthesis of coagulation factors (II, VII, IX, X, protein C, protein S) and osteocalcin (bone metabolism). Deficiency causes coagulopathy (elevated PT/INR) and impaired bone mineralization. Vitamin K antagonists (warfarin) work by inhibiting vitamin K recycling. Supplementation can reverse warfarin over-anticoagulation. Measured by LC-MS/MS from serum. K2 (menaquinone) has distinct bone and cardiovascular benefits. Optimal 0.5-2.0 ng/mL.
clinical_significance: Essential for coagulation factor synthesis and bone mineralization; deficiency causes coagulopathy; K1 reverses warfarin over-anticoagulation.

name: Zinc
text_chunk: Zinc is a cofactor for over 300 enzymes, critical for immune function, wound healing, DNA synthesis, and cell division. Deficiency affects 2 billion people globally. Serum zinc is a poor marker of total body status. Zinc is an acute phase reactant that decreases during inflammation.
clinical_significance: Essential for immune function and 300+ enzymes; deficiency impairs wound healing and immunity.
